permanentResidence.officialName: Lawful Permanent Resident (Green Card)
criteria: Have an approved immigrant petition or qualify for a self-petition category
Be admissible to the United States (pass background check and medical exam)
Have an immigrant visa number immediately available
Complete biometrics and interview at a USCIS field office or U.S. consulate
Have no disqualifying criminal history or immigration violations
Intend to reside permanently in the United States

routes.name: Student Visa (F-1) → Employment-Based Green Card Pipeline
routes.description: The most common path for international students. After graduating on an F-1 visa, students work on OPT (12 months) or STEM OPT (36 months total) while applying for the H-1B work visa lottery. Once on H-1B, an employer can sponsor an employment-based green card (EB-2 or EB-3). This multi-step pipeline typically takes 8–20+ years end-to-end and has significant bottlenecks at the H-1B lottery and EB backlog stages.
routes.estimatedDuration: 8–20+ years total: 4 years study + 1–3 years OPT + H-1B lottery attempts + employer-sponsored PERM/I-140 + visa backlog wait
routes.estimatedCost: $10,000 – $30,000+ USD (USCIS H-1B fees $500–$5,500 paid by employer; PERM + I-140 $3,000–$10,000; attorney fees $5,000–$15,000; does not include tuition)

routes.name: Employment-Based (EB-1 to EB-5)
routes.description: For foreign nationals with extraordinary ability, advanced degrees, skilled work experience, or significant investment ($800K–$1.05M). EB-1 and EB-2 NIW allow self-petition. EB-5 requires creating 10 full-time jobs.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6 months – 10+ years (depends on category and country of birth; India and China often face long backlogs)
routes.estimatedCost: $3,500 – $25,000+ USD (USCIS fees, medical exam ~$500, attorney fees $3,000–$10,000; EB-5 requires $800K–$1.05M investment)

routes.name: Family-Based Immigration
routes.description: Immediate relatives of U.S. citizens (spouse, unmarried children under 21, parents) have no visa cap. Family preference categories (F-1 through F-4) have annual limits and longer waits.
routes.estimatedDuration: 1–3 years for immediate relatives; 5–20+ years for family preference categories depending on birth country
routes.estimatedCost: $1,500 – $5,000 USD (USCIS filing fees $535–$1,440, medical exam, possible attorney fees)

routes.name: Diversity Visa Lottery (DV Program)
routes.description: Annually provides 50,000 immigrant visas to nationals of countries with historically low immigration rates to the U.S. Applicants are selected at random. Not available to nationals of high-admission countries (e.g., China, India, Mexico, Philippines).
routes.estimatedDuration: 1–2 years if selected in the lottery
routes.estimatedCost: $330 USD immigrant visa fee + travel costs (no cost to enter the lottery)

routes.name: Refugee or Asylee Status
routes.description: Individuals granted asylum or admitted as refugees may apply for a Green Card after 1 year of presence in the U.S. Must show past persecution or well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or social group.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2–7 years (asylum process itself can take 1–5 years; then 1 year before applying for PR)
routes.estimatedCost: No USCIS fee for asylees adjusting status; legal representation $2,000–$8,000

criteria: Be at least 18 years old at the time of application
Have been a Lawful Permanent Resident (Green Card holder) for at least 5 years (or 3 years if married to a U.S. citizen)
Have maintained continuous residence in the U.S. (no single trip abroad longer than 6 months)
Have been physically present in the U.S. for at least 30 months out of the last 5 years
Have lived in the state or USCIS district where you apply for at least 3 months
Be a person of good moral character during the statutory period
Demonstrate basic English reading, writing, and speaking ability
Pass a U.S. civics test (100 questions; must answer 6 of 10 correctly in the interview)
Take and pass an Oath of Allegiance to the United States

routes.name: Naturalization – General (5-Year Rule)
routes.description: The standard path to U.S. citizenship. After holding a Green Card for 5 years, file Form N-400, attend a biometrics appointment, complete an interview and civics/English test, and attend an Oath ceremony.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years as Green Card holder + 8–24 months for N-400 processing
routes.estimatedCost: $760 USD (N-400 filing fee) + optional attorney fees $1,000–$3,000

routes.name: Naturalization via Marriage to U.S. Citizen (3-Year Rule)
routes.description: Spouses of U.S. citizens who have lived in marital union for the full 3 years may apply after only 3 years as a permanent resident instead of 5.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3 years as Green Card holder + 8–18 months for N-400 processing
routes.estimatedCost: $760 USD (N-400 filing fee) + optional attorney fees

routes.name: Military Service Naturalization
routes.description: Active-duty service members or honorably discharged veterans may qualify for an expedited or fee-waived naturalization process, sometimes without meeting the full 5-year residency requirement.
routes.estimatedDuration: Varies; can be expedited to 6–12 months for active duty
routes.estimatedCost: $0 USD (filing fee waived for active-duty military)

F-1 Student.fullName: F-1 Student Visa → Permanent Residence Pipeline
F-1 Student.description: The F-1 is a nonimmigrant student visa — it does not by itself lead to a green card. However, it is the starting point for the most common immigration pipeline for international students in the U.S. The full pipeline is: F-1 → OPT or STEM OPT (work authorization) → H-1B work visa (lottery) → Employer-sponsored green card (EB-2 or EB-3) → Citizenship. Each stage has significant attrition: many students return home, fail the H-1B lottery, or get stuck in decades-long EB backlogs (especially Indian and Chinese nationals).
F-1 Student.pathToPR: F-1 Student Visa → Graduate → Apply for OPT (12 months work auth) → STEM OPT extension if eligible (24 more months = 36 total) → Enter H-1B lottery (annual, ~20–25% chance per year) → Win H-1B → Work 1–2 years → Employer files PERM labor certification → Employer files I-140 (EB-2 or EB-3) → Wait for priority date → File I-485 → Green Card
F-1 Student.pathToCitizenship: F-1 → H-1B → Green Card (EB-2/EB-3) → Live in U.S. 5 years as PR → File N-400 → Pass English + civics test → Take Oath → U.S. Citizen
F-1 Student.timelineToPR: 8–20+ years for most nationalities from starting studies. Breakdown: 2–4 years degree + 1–3 years OPT/H-1B attempts + 3–7 years employer-sponsored EB process (most nationalities). Indian nationals: 50+ year EB-2/EB-3 backlog — practically unreachable through this route without EB-1 or NIW
F-1 Student.timelineToCitizenship: 13–25+ years from starting the F-1 (most nationalities); effectively impossible within a lifetime for most Indian nationals via EB-2/EB-3
F-1 Student.probabilityNote: The 20% probability is based on multiple research sources: A National Foundation for American Policy (NFAP) study estimates ~20–25% of international students who intend to remain in the U.S. ultimately receive a green card. The pipeline has three major attrition points: (1) H-1B lottery — FY2025 selection rate was ~23% per year; over 3 STEM OPT years the cumulative chance is ~55%; (2) Employer sponsorship — only ~60% of H-1B holders receive employer-sponsored green card petitions; (3) Backlog — Indian/Chinese nationals face multi-decade waits and many abandon their applications. For non-Indian, non-Chinese nationals who win H-1B and receive employer sponsorship, the probability rises to ~55–65%. Source: NFAP analysis, USCIS H-1B lottery data, National Academies of Sciences 'The Economic and Fiscal Consequences of Immigration' (2016).

OPT / STEM OPT.fullName: OPT (Optional Practical Training) & STEM OPT Extension
OPT / STEM OPT.description: OPT is not a separate visa — it is an employment authorization benefit available to F-1 students. After completing a degree, F-1 students can apply for 12 months of OPT to work in a field directly related to their major. Students who graduated in a STEM field (science, technology, engineering, mathematics) may apply for a 24-month STEM OPT extension, giving them up to 36 months total of work authorization. OPT is the critical bridge between graduating on F-1 and transitioning to H-1B. Most students use OPT to build work experience and make H-1B lottery attempts.
OPT / STEM OPT.pathToPR: Graduate on F-1 → Apply for OPT (Form I-765) → 12 months work authorization → If STEM eligible, apply for STEM OPT extension (24 more months) → Use OPT period to apply for H-1B lottery each April → If H-1B selected, transition from OPT to H-1B → Employer sponsors green card
OPT / STEM OPT.pathToCitizenship: OPT → H-1B → Employer-sponsored EB green card → 5 years PR → Naturalization
OPT / STEM OPT.timelineToPR: OPT itself lasts 1–3 years; total time to green card from OPT start is 7–15+ years (for most nationalities)
OPT / STEM OPT.probabilityNote: The 35% probability reflects the probability from the OPT stage (post-graduation) specifically, which is higher than from the F-1 stage itself because students who reach OPT have already committed to working in the U.S. Key data points: (1) H-1B lottery: ~23% chance per annual draw; over 3 years of STEM OPT, cumulative probability of winning at least once is ~52%; (2) Among OPT workers who win H-1B, approximately 63% eventually get employer-sponsored green cards; (3) Of those who file for green cards, ~82% succeed (non-backlog countries). The STEM OPT extension (24 months) dramatically improves outcomes because it gives three lottery attempts instead of one. Source: USCIS OPT data, DHS SEVIS OPT reports, NFAP H-1B studies.

H-1B.description: The H-1B is the primary work visa for foreign nationals in specialty occupations requiring at least a bachelor's degree (or equivalent). It is the most common nonimmigrant visa that leads to a green card. Cap: 65,000 regular slots + 20,000 for U.S. master's degree or higher. Applications are submitted by employers each April for an October start. In recent years, USCIS has received 400,000–780,000 registrations for 85,000 slots, making selection highly competitive (~20–25% per year). H-1B is granted in 3-year increments, renewable up to 6 years (longer if a green card petition is pending). An H-1B holder's spouse gets an H-4 visa and may apply for H-4 EAD (work authorization) if the H-1B holder has an approved I-140.
H-1B.pathToPR: Win H-1B lottery → Employer files H-1B petition → Work on H-1B (3 years, renewable) → Employer files PERM labor certification (6–18 months) → Employer files I-140 (EB-2 or EB-3) → Wait for priority date (months to decades depending on nationality) → File I-485 → Green Card
H-1B.pathToCitizenship: H-1B → EB-2/EB-3 Green Card → 5 years PR → N-400 → Naturalization
H-1B.timelineToPR: 3–10 years for most nationalities after winning H-1B; 50+ years for Indian nationals via EB-2/EB-3 (backlog). Indian nationals should pursue EB-1A or EB-2 NIW to avoid the backlog
H-1B.timelineToCitizenship: 8–15 years for most nationalities after first H-1B approval
H-1B.probabilityNote: The 60% probability applies to individuals who have already won the H-1B lottery and are working on H-1B status. Breakdown: ~85–90% of H-1B holders who want to stay long-term find employers willing to sponsor a green card; ~82% of employer-sponsored PERM + I-140 processes succeed; ~90% of those with approved I-140s and available priority dates successfully adjust status. The overall 60% reflects that ~30–40% of H-1B holders ultimately leave the U.S. before receiving a green card (return home, fail to find sponsoring employer, or abandon due to EB backlog). Indian nationals face a uniquely severe situation: the EB-2/EB-3 India backlog now exceeds 50–100+ years, so most Indian H-1B holders cannot realistically get a green card via EB-2/EB-3 without self-petitioning via EB-1A or EB-2 NIW. Source: USCIS H-1B data, NFAP H-1B employer analysis, Congressional Research Service H-1B reports.

EB-1.description: Covers three sub-categories: EB-1A (extraordinary ability in sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics — self-petition, no employer needed), EB-1B (outstanding professors and researchers — requires employer sponsorship), and EB-1C (multinational managers or executives transferred from a foreign affiliate). This is the fastest employment-based green card path because it skips the PERM labor certification process.
EB-1.pathToPR: File I-140 petition → Wait for priority date to become current → File I-485 (or consular process) → Green Card
EB-1.pathToCitizenship: EB-1 Green Card → Live in U.S. 5 years → File N-400 → Pass civics/English test → Oath ceremony
EB-1.timelineToPR: 6 months – 2 years for most nationalities; 3–10+ years for India and China due to backlogs
EB-1.timelineToCitizenship: 5–7 years after receiving the green card (plus naturalization processing of 8–18 months)
EB-1.probabilityNote: USCIS FY2023 data shows EB-1 I-140 petition approval rates of 87–90%. The main risk factors are insufficient evidence of extraordinary ability (EB-1A) or failure to prove qualifying managerial role (EB-1C). Of all employment-based categories, EB-1 has the highest approval rate. Citizenship probability reflects the ~65% naturalization rate among eligible LPRs per DHS Yearbook of Immigration Statistics.

EB-2.description: For professionals with a U.S. advanced degree (master's or higher, or bachelor's + 5 years progressive experience) or exceptional ability in science, arts, or business. Requires employer sponsorship AND a PERM labor certification (proving no qualified U.S. workers are available) unless applying through the NIW sub-category. Large backlogs for India and China — wait times can exceed 50 years in EB-2 for Indian nationals.
EB-2.pathToPR: Employer files PERM labor certification → Employer files I-140 → Wait for priority date → File I-485 → Green Card
EB-2.pathToCitizenship: EB-2 Green Card → 5 years residence → N-400 → Naturalization
EB-2.timelineToPR: 2–5 years for most nationalities; 50+ years for India (EB-2 India backlog is currently decades long)
EB-2.timelineToCitizenship: 7–10+ years after starting the EB-2 process (most nationalities); potentially never achievable for some Indian nationals
EB-2.probabilityNote: USCIS reports EB-2 I-140 approval rates of ~83–86% for petitions filed. However, the PERM labor certification success rate is ~65–70%, so the combined end-to-end probability from PERM to green card is closer to 55–60%. The citizenship probability is lowered further because many Indian/Chinese nationals abandon their petitions due to the multi-decade backlogs. Source: USCIS I-140 data FY2023, DOL PERM statistics.

EB-2 NIW.description: A self-petition sub-category of EB-2 that waives the normal requirement for a job offer and PERM labor certification. Applicants must show their work is in a field with substantial merit and national importance, that they are well-positioned to advance that work, and that waiving the job offer requirement benefits the U.S. Highly popular among researchers, scientists, physicians (NHSC shortage areas), and engineers. No employer needed.
EB-2 NIW.pathToPR: Self-file I-140 with NIW evidence → Wait for priority date → File I-485 → Green Card
EB-2 NIW.pathToCitizenship: EB-2 NIW Green Card → 5 years residence → N-400 → Naturalization
EB-2 NIW.timelineToPR: 1–3 years for most nationalities; 50+ years for India due to shared EB-2 backlog
EB-2 NIW.probabilityNote: USCIS NIW approval rates are approximately 65–70%, lower than standard EB-2 because the 'national interest' standard is subjective and evaluated on a case-by-case basis. Approval correlates strongly with number/quality of publications, citations, and recommendation letters. USCIS AAO precedent decisions (Matter of Dhanasar, 2016) guide the standard. Source: USCIS processing data and AAO outcomes.

EB-3.fullName: EB-3: Third Preference Employment-Based (Skilled Workers & Professionals)
EB-3.description: Covers skilled workers (jobs requiring at least 2 years of training/experience), professionals (jobs requiring a U.S. bachelor's degree), and unskilled workers (other workers, jobs requiring less than 2 years training). All sub-categories require employer sponsorship and a PERM labor certification. The unskilled worker sub-category has the longest backlogs. India's EB-3 backlog exceeds 20–30 years in some sub-categories.
EB-3.pathToPR: Employer files PERM → Employer files I-140 → Wait for priority date (can be very long) → I-485 → Green Card
EB-3.pathToCitizenship: EB-3 Green Card → 5 years → N-400 → Naturalization
EB-3.timelineToPR: 2–5 years for most nationalities; 20–30+ years for India; 5–10 years for China
EB-3.probabilityNote: USCIS EB-3 I-140 approval rate is ~80–84%. However, considering PERM success (~65%) and the high abandonment rate among Indian nationals due to the multi-decade backlog, the practical end-to-end probability is significantly lower. Many Indian-born applicants downgrade from EB-2 to EB-3 to take advantage of a slightly faster (but still very long) queue. Citizenship probability is adjusted for the substantial backlog-driven abandonment. Source: USCIS FY2023 Annual Report.

EB-4.description: A catch-all category for specific immigrant types: religious workers (ministers, religious workers for nonprofit religious organizations), certain broadcasters (VOA, Radio Free Europe), U.S. government employees abroad, and Special Immigrant Juvenile Status (SIJS) for abused/neglected/abandoned minors, among others. Religious workers must have been carrying out the religious vocation for 2 years before applying. Annual cap of approximately 9,940 visas.
EB-4.pathToPR: File I-360 petition (or other relevant form) → File I-485 → Green Card
EB-4.pathToCitizenship: EB-4 Green Card → 5 years → N-400 → Naturalization
EB-4.timelineToPR: 1–4 years depending on sub-category and country
EB-4.probabilityNote: EB-4 approval rates vary significantly by sub-category. Religious worker I-360 petitions have an approval rate of ~70–75%. SIJS has higher approval rates (~85%) when properly documented. The lower overall probability reflects the complexity of the niche eligibility requirements and the relatively small annual visa cap, which creates some backlog. Source: USCIS I-360 processing data.

EB-5.description: For foreign nationals who invest $1,050,000 (or $800,000 in a Targeted Employment Area — rural or high unemployment) in a new U.S. commercial enterprise that creates at least 10 full-time jobs for U.S. workers. Can also invest through USCIS-designated Regional Centers. Initially granted a 2-year conditional green card; must file I-829 to remove conditions and receive permanent green card.
EB-5.pathToPR: File I-526E petition → Conditional Green Card (2 years) → File I-829 to remove conditions → Permanent Green Card
EB-5.pathToCitizenship: EB-5 Green Card → 5 years → N-400 → Naturalization
EB-5.timelineToPR: 2–5 years for most nationalities; 5–10+ years for China (China EB-5 backlog exists)
EB-5.probabilityNote: EB-5 I-526/I-526E approval rates historically ranged from 70–80% but dropped with increased USCIS scrutiny following the 2022 EB-5 Reform Act. I-829 (removing conditions) has a higher approval rate (~88%) for investors who maintained their investment and created the required jobs. The lower overall probability reflects the complexity of maintaining job-creation documentation and source-of-funds requirements. Source: USCIS I-526 processing data.

IR-1.fullName: IR-1: Immediate Relative – Spouse of a U.S. Citizen
IR-1.description: The most common path to a green card. If you are the spouse of a U.S. citizen and have been married for 2+ years at time of visa issuance, you receive an immediate, unconditional green card (IR-1). If married less than 2 years, you receive a conditional green card (CR-1) valid for 2 years, then must file I-751 to remove conditions. No visa cap — there is no waiting list for spouses of U.S. citizens.
IR-1.pathToPR: USC spouse files I-130 → NVC processing → Consular interview (or I-485 if already in U.S.) → Green Card
IR-1.pathToCitizenship: IR-1 Green Card → 3 years as PR while married to USC → N-400 → Naturalization (3-year rule applies)
IR-1.timelineToPR: 12–24 months (consular processing); 8–18 months (adjustment of status inside U.S.)
IR-1.timelineToCitizenship: 3 years as PR + 8–18 months N-400 processing = approximately 5–7 years total
IR-1.probabilityNote: IR-1/CR-1 petitions have approval rates of ~94–96% — the highest of any green card category. The primary denial reasons are proof of bona fide marriage (most common), inadmissibility due to prior immigration violations, or criminal history. Spouses of USCs can apply for naturalization after only 3 years of PR status (vs. 5 years for others), making this the fastest path to citizenship. Source: USCIS I-130 approval data FY2023.

IR-2.fullName: IR-2: Immediate Relative – Unmarried Child Under 21 of a U.S. Citizen
IR-2.description: Covers unmarried children (biological, step, or adopted) under 21 years of age whose parent is a U.S. citizen. As an immediate relative, there is no annual visa cap and no waiting list. The Child Status Protection Act (CSPA) provides some protection against aging out to 21 during long petition processing.
IR-2.pathToPR: USC parent files I-130 → NVC or I-485 → Green Card
IR-2.pathToCitizenship: IR-2 Green Card → 5 years as PR → N-400 → Naturalization (or may derive citizenship automatically if under 18 when parent naturalizes)
IR-2.timelineToCitizenship: 5 years as PR + ~1 year processing = ~7 years total (or automatic derivation if parent naturalizes while child is under 18)
IR-2.probabilityNote: IR-2 petitions have approval rates of ~93–95%. Denial reasons are typically inability to prove parent-child relationship, prior immigration violations, or the child turning 21 during processing (aging out — partially mitigated by CSPA). Adopted children must meet additional requirements under the Hague Convention or IR-3/IR-4 categories. Source: USCIS I-130 data.

IR-5.fullName: IR-5: Immediate Relative – Parent of a U.S. Citizen (21 or older)
IR-5.description: Allows U.S. citizens aged 21 or older to sponsor one or both parents for a green card. Both biological and adoptive parents qualify. No annual visa cap. Parents who obtain a green card through an adult child are NOT eligible to then sponsor other relatives (they receive a 'limited' green card for this purpose in practice).
IR-5.pathToPR: Adult USC child files I-130 → NVC or I-485 → Green Card
IR-5.pathToCitizenship: IR-5 Green Card → 5 years as PR → N-400 → Naturalization
IR-5.timelineToCitizenship: 5 years PR + ~1 year processing = ~7 years total
IR-5.probabilityNote: IR-5 petitions have approval rates of ~90–92%. Denial is usually due to proof-of-relationship issues, inadmissibility (prior unlawful presence, health grounds), or fraud. Approval rates are slightly lower than IR-1/IR-2 because older applicants may face more medical inadmissibility issues. Parents are not permitted to then petition for siblings or other relatives beyond what is allowed. Source: USCIS data.

F-1 Pref.fullName: F-1: Family First Preference – Unmarried Adult Sons & Daughters of U.S. Citizens
F-1 Pref.description: Note: this is NOT the F-1 student visa. This is the family first preference immigrant category for unmarried sons and daughters of U.S. citizens who are 21 years of age or older. Subject to an annual visa cap (~23,400 visas/year). If an IR-2 beneficiary ages out (turns 21), they typically fall down to F-1 preference. Wait times vary by birth country — currently 3–7+ years.
F-1 Pref.pathToPR: USC parent files I-130 → Wait for visa availability (priority date) → NVC or I-485 → Green Card
F-1 Pref.pathToCitizenship: F-1 Pref Green Card → 5 years PR → N-400 → Naturalization
F-1 Pref.timelineToPR: 3–8 years for most nationalities; longer for Mexico and Philippines
F-1 Pref.probabilityNote: F-1 family preference approval rates are ~77–80% once a visa number becomes available. The lower overall probability compared to immediate relatives reflects abandonment during the long wait period — applicants may marry (making them F-3, not F-1), the USC parent may pass away, or the applicant may find another immigration path. Source: USCIS I-130 data and Visa Bulletin wait time analysis.

F-2A.fullName: F-2A: Family Second Preference (A) – Spouses and Unmarried Children Under 21 of LPRs
F-2A.description: For spouses and unmarried children (under 21) of Lawful Permanent Residents (green card holders, not citizens). This is distinct from IR-1 (spouse of a U.S. citizen). Annual cap of ~87,900 for F-2A/F-2B combined. Wait times are generally 2–3 years. When the sponsoring LPR naturalizes, beneficiaries automatically upgrade to immediate relative status (no cap, faster processing).
F-2A.pathToPR: LPR sponsor files I-130 → Wait for visa availability → NVC or I-485 → Green Card
F-2A.pathToCitizenship: F-2A Green Card → 5 years PR (or 3 years if sponsored spouse naturalizes and you marry) → N-400
F-2A.probabilityNote: F-2A petitions have approval rates of ~84–86%. The wait time is moderate (2–4 years) and beneficiaries automatically upgrade to immediate relative status if the LPR sponsor naturalizes, which removes the cap and accelerates the process. The probability is meaningfully higher than other preference categories due to the relatively short wait and automatic upgrade option. Source: USCIS FY2023 data.

F-2B.fullName: F-2B: Family Second Preference (B) – Unmarried Adult Sons & Daughters of LPRs
F-2B.description: For unmarried sons and daughters aged 21 or older whose parent is a Lawful Permanent Resident. Annual cap shared with F-2A (~87,900 combined). Wait times are longer than F-2A, currently 4–7 years for most countries. Unlike F-2A, beneficiaries cannot automatically upgrade to immediate relative status because they are adults.
F-2B.pathToPR: LPR parent files I-130 → Wait for priority date (4–7 years) → Green Card
F-2B.pathToCitizenship: F-2B Green Card → 5 years PR → N-400 → Naturalization
F-2B.timelineToPR: 4–8 years for most nationalities; longer for Mexico and Philippines
F-2B.probabilityNote: F-2B approval rates are ~72–75% once a visa is available. The lower probability compared to F-2A reflects the longer wait period during which applicants may: move abroad, marry (disqualifying them from F-2B entirely), find another path, or abandon the petition. Marriage during the wait drops the applicant to F-3 (married adult child of USC) or no eligibility if LPR parent. Source: USCIS I-130 outcome data.

F-3.fullName: F-3: Family Third Preference – Married Sons & Daughters of U.S. Citizens
F-3.description: For married sons and daughters (of any age) of U.S. citizens. Both the principal applicant and their spouse/children are included. Annual cap of ~23,400 visas. Wait times are long — typically 7–15 years for most nationalities, and over 20 years for Mexico and Philippines. A very small quota relative to demand makes this one of the slower family preference categories.
F-3.pathToPR: USC parent files I-130 → Long wait for priority date → NVC or I-485 → Green Card
F-3.pathToCitizenship: F-3 Green Card → 5 years PR → N-400 → Naturalization
F-3.timelineToPR: 7–15 years for most countries; 20+ years for Mexico and Philippines
F-3.probabilityNote: F-3 petitions have approval rates of ~63–67% once a visa becomes available. The significantly lower probability reflects the very long wait period (7–15+ years) during which life circumstances change drastically: petitioners die, marriages end in divorce (disqualifying the beneficiary entirely), or beneficiaries find other paths. The annual quota of ~23,400 is insufficient for the demand, particularly from Mexico, Philippines, India, and China. Source: USCIS data and Visa Bulletin historical analysis.

F-4.fullName: F-4: Family Fourth Preference – Brothers & Sisters of U.S. Citizens
F-4.description: The last and most oversubscribed family preference category. For brothers and sisters of U.S. citizens where the USC is at least 21. Annual cap of only ~65,000 visas. Wait times are extreme: currently 13–25+ years for most nationalities, and over 25 years for Mexico, India, and Philippines. The long wait means it is often impractical for many applicants.
F-4.pathToPR: Adult USC sibling files I-130 → Very long wait for priority date → NVC or I-485 → Green Card
F-4.pathToCitizenship: F-4 Green Card → 5 years PR → N-400 → Naturalization
F-4.probabilityNote: F-4 has the lowest success rate of all family preference categories. While I-130 approval rates are ~82–85%, the probability from initial petition filing to receiving a green card drops significantly due to the extremely long waits (13–25+ years). Studies show that only about 55% of approved F-4 petitions ultimately result in a visa, as petitioners pass away, USC sponsors renounce citizenship or pass away, or beneficiaries abandon the petition. Source: USCIS, Cato Institute immigration studies.

DV Visa.fullName: DV Visa: Diversity Immigrant Visa (Green Card Lottery)
DV Visa.description: An annual lottery program providing 50,000 immigrant visas to nationals of countries with historically low immigration to the United States. Applicants from high-immigration countries are excluded (including China, India, Mexico, Philippines, Brazil, Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala, Haiti, Honduras, Jamaica, Nigeria, Pakistan, South Korea, United Kingdom, and Vietnam). The application (called the DV Entry) is free and open every October–November. Selectees undergo a full immigrant visa process.
DV Visa.pathToPR: Enter free DV Lottery (October–November) → If selected, complete online application → Consular interview → Immigrant visa → Enter U.S. → Green Card
DV Visa.pathToCitizenship: DV Green Card → 5 years PR → N-400 → Naturalization
DV Visa.timelineToPR: 1–2 years from selection notification to receiving green card
DV Visa.probabilityNote: The probability of 2% represents the chance that any given lottery entry results in a green card. Approximately 22 million people enter the DV lottery annually for 55,000 slots (~50,000 immigrants + ~5,000 lottery buffer for no-shows). The raw selection rate is about 0.25–0.5%, but since eligible countries vary and some entries are disqualified, the effective probability for eligible applicants is slightly higher. Of those selected, approximately 90% complete the process and receive a green card. Source: U.S. Department of State Bureau of Consular Affairs, DV Program statistics.

Asylum.fullName: Asylum – Protection from Persecution in the U.S.
Asylum.description: Asylum allows individuals already in the United States (or at a port of entry) to request protection if they have suffered persecution or have a well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, membership in a particular social group, or political opinion. Two types: Affirmative asylum (filed with USCIS if not in removal proceedings, interview with asylum officer) and Defensive asylum (raised as a defense in immigration court during removal proceedings). After 1 year as an asylee, you may apply for a green card.
Asylum.pathToPR: File Form I-589 (Asylum Application) → USCIS interview or immigration court hearing → If granted asylum, wait 1 year → File I-485 → Green Card
Asylum.pathToCitizenship: Asylum → 1 year wait → Green Card → 4 more years as PR (total 5) → N-400 → Naturalization
Asylum.timelineToPR: 3–8 years total (asylum backlog is currently 1–5 years; then 1 year wait before PR application; then I-485 processing)
Asylum.probabilityNote: USCIS grants affirmative asylum to approximately 36–40% of applicants. Immigration court approval rates (defensive asylum) average around 55%, but range from 10–90% depending on the immigration judge, nationality, and case type. Combined across both tracks, roughly 32% of all asylum applicants ultimately receive asylum and proceed to a green card. The probability is highly sensitive to country of origin and legal representation — applicants with attorneys are 5x more likely to win. Source: USCIS Asylum Division data, TRAC Immigration Court statistics.

Refugee.fullName: Refugee Status – Resettlement in the United States
Refugee.description: Unlike asylum (applied from within the U.S.), refugee status is applied from abroad through the U.S. Refugee Admissions Program (USRAP). Candidates are typically referred by the UNHCR, a U.S. Embassy, or an NGO. The U.S. conducts extensive security vetting overseas before admission. Annual refugee admission caps are set by the President (FY2024: 125,000). After 1 year of continuous presence in the U.S. as a refugee, you are required (not optional) to apply for a green card.
Refugee.pathToPR: UNHCR referral or direct access → USCIS overseas interview → Extensive security screening → Admission to U.S. as refugee → 1 year mandatory wait → File I-730 or I-485 → Green Card
Refugee.pathToCitizenship: Refugee admission → 1 year → Green Card → 4 more years PR (total 5 years from admission) → N-400 → Naturalization
Refugee.timelineToPR: 2–5 years from initial referral to green card (overseas processing 1–3 years + 1 year in U.S.)
Refugee.timelineToCitizenship: 5+ years after U.S. admission (counting 1 year before PR + 4 years as PR, or 5 years total PR)
Refugee.probabilityNote: Once admitted to the U.S. as a refugee, approximately 85–88% eventually adjust to permanent resident status. The smaller percentage who don't adjust includes those who return voluntarily to their home country, are deported for criminal activity, or fail to file in time. The USCIS refugee I-485 adjustment approval rate is ~95% for those who do file. Notably, refugees have the strongest path to long-term immigration status despite the difficult initial process. Source: USCIS refugee adjustment of status data, DHS Yearbook.

CR-1.fullName: CR-1: Conditional Resident – Spouse of U.S. Citizen (Marriage Under 2 Years)
CR-1.description: When a U.S. citizen petitions for their foreign spouse and the couple has been married for less than 2 years at the time the visa is issued, the spouse receives a CR-1 conditional green card (valid 2 years) instead of the IR-1 permanent green card. Within 90 days before the 2-year anniversary of receiving the green card, the couple must jointly file I-751 (Petition to Remove Conditions on Residence) to prove the marriage was entered in good faith. If approved, a 10-year permanent green card is issued.
CR-1.pathToPR: USC spouse files I-130 → CR-1 issued (2-year conditional) → File I-751 within 90 days before 2-year mark → 10-year permanent green card
CR-1.pathToCitizenship: CR-1 → Remove conditions (I-751) → Permanent Green Card → 3 years total PR (3-year marriage rule) → N-400 → Naturalization
CR-1.timelineToPR: 12–24 months to get CR-1 + 2 years conditional period + I-751 processing (12–24 months) = total 4–5 years to permanent green card
CR-1.timelineToCitizenship: 3 years as PR (while married to USC) + 8–18 months N-400 = ~5–7 years from first entering U.S.
CR-1.probabilityNote: CR-1 petitions have a similar approval rate to IR-1 (~94–96%). However, the I-751 removal of conditions step adds a second hurdle — USCIS approves approximately 93–95% of jointly filed I-751s. The primary risk factor is marriage termination during the conditional period, which triggers scrutiny. Waivers are available for abuse victims, death of the sponsoring spouse, or extreme hardship. Source: USCIS I-751 processing data.

Green Card (any category).fullName: Lawful Permanent Resident (Green Card) – General Naturalization
Green Card (any category).description: To naturalize under the standard 5-year rule, you must have held a green card (any category) for at least 5 continuous years, been physically present in the U.S. for at least 30 months of those 5 years, and maintained the U.S. as your primary residence. The N-400 application includes biometrics, an English language interview, and a civics test (6 of 10 questions from a 100-question bank).
Green Card (any category).pathToPR: N/A — you must already be a Permanent Resident to follow this path.
Green Card (any category).pathToCitizenship: Hold Green Card 5 years → File N-400 → Biometrics → Interview (English + civics test) → Oath of Allegiance ceremony → U.S. Citizen
Green Card (any category).timelineToCitizenship: 8–24 months from filing N-400 (processing times vary significantly by field office)
Green Card (any category).probabilityNote: USCIS approves approximately 90–92% of N-400 naturalization applications filed. The primary denial reasons are failure to pass the English or civics test (applicants can re-take once), failing to demonstrate continuous residence, criminal history discovered during background check, or failure to pay taxes. Of the ~900,000+ N-400 applications filed annually, about 90% result in citizenship. Source: USCIS N-400 outcome data FY2023.

Military Service.fullName: Military Service Naturalization – Active Duty & Veterans
Military Service.description: U.S. law provides expedited naturalization for non-citizen members of the U.S. Armed Forces serving honorably. Under INA §328, those who have served honorably for 1+ year may apply for naturalization without meeting the standard 5-year PR requirement (but still need PR status). Under INA §329, those serving in a designated period of hostilities (e.g., post-9/11) may naturalize immediately upon honorable service, even without being a PR first. Filing fees are waived for active-duty service members.
Military Service.pathToPR: Some military members naturalize directly from nonimmigrant status during hostilities (INA §329) — making this the only path to citizenship that may bypass the PR stage entirely.
Military Service.pathToCitizenship: Honorable military service → File Form N-426 (certified by commanding officer) + N-400 → Expedited processing → Oath → U.S. Citizen
Military Service.timelineToPR: N/A (may bypass PR entirely for INA §329 cases during hostilities)
Military Service.timelineToCitizenship: 3–12 months (expedited processing; can be done overseas at a U.S. consulate)
Military Service.probabilityNote: Military naturalization applications have one of the highest approval rates of any naturalization category (~91–93%). USCIS and USCIS military liaisons prioritize these cases. Denial is uncommon and typically involves disqualifying criminal history or fraudulent service records. Approximately 8,000–10,000 members of the military naturalize annually. Source: USCIS military naturalization data, DHS Yearbook of Immigration Statistics.

permanentResidence.officialName: Canadian Permanent Resident (PR Card)
criteria: Meet the specific eligibility requirements of your chosen immigration program
Demonstrate language proficiency in English or French (minimum CLB 7 for most Express Entry streams)
Have a valid job offer, sufficient Canadian work experience, or qualifying education
Have proof of sufficient settlement funds (if not already working in Canada)
Pass criminal background checks (RCMP and home country)
Pass a medical examination
Have no immigration-related inadmissibility issues

routes.name: Study Permit → PGWP → Express Entry Pipeline
routes.description: The most common path for international students in Canada. After graduating from a Designated Learning Institution (DLI), students apply for a Post-Graduation Work Permit (PGWP) — no lottery, no employer required. After 1 year of skilled work on PGWP, graduates qualify for Express Entry's Canadian Experience Class (CEC), which awards bonus CRS points for Canadian education and experience. Unlike the U.S. H-1B route, there is no annual lottery and no employer sponsorship needed for PR.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5–9 years total: 2–4 years study + 1–3 years PGWP work + 6–12 months Express Entry processing

routes.name: Express Entry – Federal Skilled Worker (FSW)
routes.description: Points-based system (Comprehensive Ranking System) for skilled workers outside Canada. Score is based on age, education, language, work experience, and adaptability. IRCC issues Invitations to Apply (ITA) in regular draws.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–12 months from Invitation to Apply (ITA) to receiving PR
routes.estimatedCost: $1,365 CAD principal applicant + $1,365 CAD per accompanying adult + $230 CAD per child + Right of Permanent Residence fee $515 CAD + settlement funds (varies)

routes.name: Express Entry – Canadian Experience Class (CEC)
routes.description: For people who already have at least 1 year of skilled work experience inside Canada and meet language requirements. Typically achieves higher CRS scores due to Canadian experience bonus.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6 months from ITA to PR
routes.estimatedCost: Same as FSW: ~$3,000–$4,000 CAD total (government fees + biometrics)

routes.name: Provincial Nominee Program (PNP)
routes.description: Each province and territory nominates candidates based on local labor market needs. A provincial nomination adds 600 CRS points in Express Entry (virtually guaranteeing an ITA) or follows a separate base process.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–18 months (provincial nomination 3–6 months + federal PR processing 6–12 months)
routes.estimatedCost: $500–$1,500 CAD provincial application fee + federal PR fees (~$3,000 CAD)

routes.name: Family Sponsorship
routes.description: Canadian citizens or PRs can sponsor a spouse, common-law partner, dependent children, or parents/grandparents. Sponsor must meet minimum income requirements. Parents/grandparents subject to annual intake cap.
routes.estimatedDuration: 12 months for spouses/partners; 20–36 months for parents/grandparents
routes.estimatedCost: $1,080 CAD (sponsor + principal applicant combined fee) + $515 CAD Right of PR fee + biometrics

routes.name: Start-up Visa Program
routes.description: For entrepreneurs with an innovative business concept supported by a designated Canadian venture capital fund, angel investor group, or business incubator. Business must be incorporated in Canada.
routes.estimatedDuration: 12–36 months
routes.estimatedCost: ~$1,575 CAD government fees + significant legal/consulting fees ($5,000–$20,000 CAD)

routes.name: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP)
routes.description: Employer-driven program for Atlantic provinces (New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, PEI, Newfoundland). Requires a job offer from a designated employer and settlement plan. Pathways for international graduates too.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–12 months
routes.estimatedCost: ~$2,500–$4,000 CAD (government fees similar to Express Entry)

criteria: Be a permanent resident of Canada
Have been physically present in Canada for at least 1,095 days (3 years) out of the last 5 years
Days as a temporary resident or protected person before becoming a PR count as half-days (max 365 days credit)
Have filed Canadian income taxes for at least 3 years during the 5-year period (if required to do so)
Demonstrate adequate knowledge of English or French (CLB/NCLC 4 or higher)
Pass the citizenship knowledge test (20 questions about Canada's history, values, and government; must score at least 15/20)
Be 18 or older at time of application (minors can be included in a parent's application)
Have no prohibitions such as an active criminal sentence or removal order
Take the Oath of Citizenship at a citizenship ceremony

routes.name: Naturalization after Permanent Residence (3-Year Physical Presence)
routes.description: The standard path to Canadian citizenship. After accumulating 1,095 days of physical presence in Canada within the last 5 years as a PR (or partly as a temporary resident), you may apply for citizenship online. Complete a knowledge test, language assessment, and attend a ceremony.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3 years of qualifying presence + 12–24 months for application processing
routes.estimatedCost: $630 CAD per adult applicant, $100 CAD per minor (under 18)

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born Abroad to Canadian Parent)
routes.description: Persons born outside Canada to a Canadian citizen parent who was themselves born in Canada or naturalized may be a Canadian citizen by descent. Limited to the first generation born abroad. Apply for a citizenship certificate to confirm status.
routes.estimatedDuration: Immediate entitlement; certificate processing 12–18 months

Study Permit.fullName: Canadian Study Permit → Permanent Residence Pipeline
Study Permit.description: The Study Permit is Canada's student visa, issued by IRCC for international students attending a Designated Learning Institution (DLI). It does not directly lead to PR, but it is the starting point for one of the clearest and most accessible student-to-PR pipelines in the world. Unlike the U.S. F-1 route (which requires winning an H-1B lottery), Canada's route is lottery-free: graduate → get a PGWP automatically → work 1 year → apply to Express Entry CEC → receive PR in 6 months. Canada has explicitly stated a policy goal of converting international students to permanent residents, and regularly targets international graduates in Express Entry draws.
Study Permit.pathToPR: Study Permit → Graduate from DLI → Apply for PGWP (open work permit, no employer needed) → Work 1+ year in NOC TEER 0/1/2/3 occupation → Meet language requirement (CLB 7) → Create Express Entry profile (CEC stream) → Receive ITA → Submit PR application → Receive PR within 6 months
Study Permit.pathToCitizenship: Study Permit → PGWP → PR (via CEC/Express Entry) → Accumulate 1,095 days physical presence in Canada over 5 years → Apply for citizenship → Pass knowledge test → Oath ceremony → Canadian Citizen
Study Permit.timelineToPR: 5–9 years from starting studies: 2–4 years degree + 1–3 years PGWP work + 6–12 months Express Entry processing
Study Permit.timelineToCitizenship: 8–13 years from starting studies (3 more years of physical presence after PR + 12–24 months citizenship processing)
Study Permit.probabilityNote: The 38% probability is based on IRCC data showing that approximately 35–40% of international students who graduate from Canadian DLIs eventually obtain Canadian permanent residence. This is significantly higher than the U.S. F-1 → PR rate (~20%) for three key reasons: (1) No lottery — PGWP is granted automatically to eligible graduates with near-100% approval rate; (2) No employer sponsorship required for Express Entry CEC; (3) Canadian education and work experience add CRS points, making draws more accessible. IRCC reported that in 2022–2023, international students accounted for over 40% of new PR admissions in some quarters. The 38% reflects total attrition including students who return home, fail language tests, or don't accumulate enough CRS points. Source: IRCC Annual Report 2022–2023, Statistics Canada international student data, IRCC Express Entry Year-End Report.

FSW (CRS Points-Based).fullName: Express Entry – Federal Skilled Worker (FSW) Program
FSW (CRS Points-Based).description: The FSW program selects skilled workers from outside Canada through the Express Entry system using a points-based Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS). Points are awarded for age, education, language proficiency (English and/or French), work experience, and adaptability factors (spouse's qualifications, Canadian siblings, etc.). IRCC runs regular 'draws' inviting candidates above a CRS cutoff. There are no employer or job offer requirements for FSW, though a valid job offer adds 50–200 bonus CRS points. FSW is the primary pathway for internationally trained professionals who have not yet lived or worked in Canada.
FSW (CRS Points-Based).pathToPR: Assess eligibility (NOC skill level, language, education) → Get credentials assessed → Take IELTS/CELPIP (CLB 7 minimum) → Create Express Entry profile → Enter FSW pool → Receive ITA when CRS score meets draw cutoff → Submit complete PR application within 60 days → Background check + medical exam → Receive COPR → Land as PR
FSW (CRS Points-Based).pathToCitizenship: FSW PR → Live in Canada (accumulate 1,095 days in 5 years) → File citizenship application → Pass citizenship knowledge test → Oath ceremony → Canadian Citizen
FSW (CRS Points-Based).timelineToPR: 6–18 months from creating an Express Entry profile to receiving PR (if CRS score is competitive); preparation time (language testing, credential assessment) adds 3–6 months beforehand
FSW (CRS Points-Based).timelineToCitizenship: 3 years of qualifying presence after PR + 12–24 months processing = ~5–6 years after receiving PR
FSW (CRS Points-Based).probabilityNote: The 72% probability represents the chance that an FSW-eligible applicant who creates an Express Entry profile eventually receives a PR. The main attrition points are: (1) CRS score not competitive enough to receive an ITA in regular draws (~20% of pool candidates are never invited); (2) Application rejection due to misrepresentation, medical, or criminal inadmissibility (~5%); (3) Abandonment during the 60-day application window. Candidates with French language skills, Canadian job offers, or sibling connections have substantially higher success rates due to bonus CRS points. In 2023, IRCC introduced category-based draws (STEM, healthcare, trades, French, agriculture) which increased invitation rates for targeted groups. Source: IRCC Express Entry Year-End Report 2022–2023, IRCC Operational Instructions.

Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.fullName: Temporary Foreign Worker Permit (LMIA-Based Work Permit)
Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.description: A Temporary Foreign Worker (TFW) permit allows Canadian employers to hire foreign nationals for specific jobs when no qualified Canadian workers are available. The employer must obtain a Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) from Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC), which verifies the genuine need for a foreign worker. The permit is employer-specific and job-specific — workers cannot change employers without a new LMIA. However, after gaining 1 year of skilled Canadian work experience (NOC TEER 0, 1, 2, or 3), TFW holders become eligible to apply for PR through Express Entry's Canadian Experience Class (CEC).
Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.pathToPR: Employer obtains LMIA → Employer-specific work permit issued → Work 1+ year in skilled NOC TEER 0/1/2/3 role → Meet CLB 7 language requirement → Submit Express Entry CEC profile → Receive ITA → Submit PR application → Receive PR
Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.pathToCitizenship: TFW Permit → CEC PR → 1,095 days physical presence → Citizenship application → Test + Oath → Canadian Citizen
Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.timelineToPR: 1–3 years to gain CEC eligibility after arriving on TFW + 6–12 months Express Entry processing = 2–4 years total
Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.timelineToCitizenship: 5–8 years from TFW permit to citizenship
Temporary Foreign Worker Permit.probabilityNote: The 42% probability reflects the subset of TFW permit holders who successfully transition to PR. Key attrition factors: (1) Many TFW holders work in lower-skilled roles (TEER 4/5) that don't qualify for CEC — only skilled TEER 0–3 experience counts; (2) Language requirements (CLB 7) exclude workers in sectors that don't require strong English/French; (3) Employer-tied status means job loss can trigger departure before meeting CEC requirements; (4) Some TFW holders find other PR pathways (PNP, RNIP) not captured here. Workers in healthcare, trades, and IT on TFW permits have significantly higher conversion rates (~65–70%) due to higher-skilled roles and targeted PNP streams. Source: IRCC TFW program statistics, ESDC LMIA data, Statistics Canada Labour Force Survey.

PNP Stream.fullName: Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) – Province or Territory Stream
PNP Stream.description: Each Canadian province and territory (except Quebec and Nunavut) operates its own Provincial Nominee Program with multiple streams targeting workers, graduates, entrepreneurs, and semi-skilled workers based on local labor market needs. There are two broad types: (1) Enhanced PNP — aligned with Express Entry; a provincial nomination adds 600 CRS points, virtually guaranteeing an ITA; (2) Base PNP — separate from Express Entry, follows a provincial application process. Notable streams include Ontario's Human Capital Priorities Stream, BC's Skills Immigration, Alberta's Opportunity Stream, and Saskatchewan's International Skilled Worker category. Some provinces specifically target international graduates from their own institutions.
PNP Stream.pathToPR: Identify target province and eligible stream → Submit Expression of Interest (EOI) to province → Receive provincial nomination certificate → If Enhanced PNP: +600 CRS points in Express Entry → Guaranteed ITA → Submit federal PR application → Receive PR. If Base PNP: Submit federal PR application directly after nomination → Receive PR
PNP Stream.pathToCitizenship: PNP PR → Settle in Canada (1,095 days over 5 years) → Citizenship application → Knowledge test → Oath → Canadian Citizen
PNP Stream.timelineToPR: 6–18 months total: provincial stream processing 3–6 months + federal PR processing 6–12 months
PNP Stream.probabilityNote: The 78% probability reflects the end-to-end likelihood from applying to a PNP stream to receiving federal PR. Provincial nomination approval rates vary by stream: job-offer streams have ~85% approval; human capital streams have ~70–75% depending on score competitiveness. Once a provincial nomination is received, the federal IRCC PR approval rate is ~94%. The combined probability accounts for applicants who apply to PNP but don't meet provincial thresholds. Note: Quebec operates its own selection system (QSWP, PEQ) with different criteria. Some PNP streams — particularly in Atlantic provinces and rural areas — have much higher success rates due to strong labor demand and lower competition. Source: IRCC PNP data, provincial government immigration statistics.

Spousal Sponsorship.description: Canadian citizens and Permanent Residents who are at least 18 years old may sponsor their spouse, common-law partner (cohabiting 12+ months), or conjugal partner (12+ months relationship with legal barriers to cohabitation). The sponsor must prove the relationship is genuine and not entered primarily for immigration purposes. If the sponsored partner is already in Canada on a valid status, they can apply simultaneously for a work permit while the PR is processing (facilitated pathway). If outside Canada, the application is processed through a Canadian visa office abroad. Canada does not have a separate 'conditional' PR like the U.S. CR-1 — sponsored spouses receive a full 5-year PR card directly.
Spousal Sponsorship.pathToPR: Sponsor submits sponsorship application + Partner submits PR application → IRCC assesses sponsor eligibility → If in Canada: interim open work permit issued → Background check + medical + interview (if required) → Full PR card issued
Spousal Sponsorship.pathToCitizenship: Spousal PR → Accumulate 1,095 days in Canada over 5 years → Citizenship application → Test + Oath → Canadian Citizen. (No 3-year rule like the U.S. — Canada does not reduce the PR requirement for spouses of citizens)
Spousal Sponsorship.timelineToPR: 12–24 months for inland applications (partner in Canada); 12–18 months for outland applications (partner outside Canada)
Spousal Sponsorship.timelineToCitizenship: 3 years of PR presence + 12–24 months processing = ~5–6 years total from PR
Spousal Sponsorship.probabilityNote: IRCC approves approximately 89–93% of spousal/common-law sponsorship applications. The primary reasons for refusal are: (1) Relationship not deemed genuine (most common — ~5% of applications); (2) Sponsor financial inadmissibility or prior sponsorship undertaking violations; (3) Applicant criminal or medical inadmissibility. Canada does not require a sponsor income floor for spouses (unlike parents/grandparents), making spousal sponsorship more accessible. Note: unlike the U.S., Canada does not issue a 'conditional' PR — spouses receive a full PR card immediately. Source: IRCC spousal sponsorship program data, IRCC Annual Report.

Child Sponsorship.description: Canadian citizens and PRs can sponsor their dependent children for permanent residence. A 'dependent child' is under 22 years of age AND does not have a spouse or common-law partner, OR is 22 or older and has depended substantially on the parent's financial support since before age 22 due to a mental or physical condition. Biological children, adopted children, and step-children may all qualify. There is no annual cap or lottery for child sponsorship. Adopted children may also be sponsored as Convention Refugees if they were adopted under specific circumstances.
Child Sponsorship.pathToPR: Canadian citizen/PR parent files sponsorship + child files PR application → Document review (birth certificate, proof of relationship, adoption records if applicable) → Medical exam → Background check → PR approved → Child lands as PR
Child Sponsorship.pathToCitizenship: Child PR → 1,095 days presence in Canada → Citizenship application (or can be included in parent's citizenship application if under 18 and parent is naturalizing)
Child Sponsorship.timelineToCitizenship: 3 years of qualifying presence + processing = ~4–5 years from PR (or automatic if included in parent's application before age 18)
Child Sponsorship.probabilityNote: Dependent child sponsorship has one of the highest PR approval rates in Canadian immigration at ~93–95%. The main denial reasons are inability to prove the parent-child relationship (particularly for adopted children where legal documentation is complex), the child aging out during processing (turning 22), or the child entering a relationship that makes them no longer 'dependent.' IRCC processes child sponsorships with relatively high priority. Children who are included in a parent's citizenship application can become Canadian citizens before age 18 without needing to independently meet the physical presence requirement. Source: IRCC family class immigration data.

Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).fullName: Parent and Grandparent Program (PGP) – Annual Intake
Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).description: The PGP allows Canadian citizens and PRs to sponsor their parents and grandparents for permanent residence. Due to extremely high demand, IRCC uses an annual randomized intake process (interest to sponsor): sponsors submit an expression of interest during a limited window (usually a few weeks in January), and IRCC randomly selects applicants up to the annual cap (typically 15,000–30,000 spaces per year). Only those selected are invited to submit a full sponsorship application. The sponsor must meet minimum income requirements (LICO + 30%) for 3 consecutive years. As an alternative, parents who don't get PGP can apply for the Super Visa (10-year multi-entry visitor visa allowing 5-year stays).
Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).pathToPR: Submit expression of interest during intake window (January) → If randomly selected, receive invitation to apply → Submit full sponsorship and PR application → IRCC assessment → Background check + medical + interview → PR approved
Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).pathToCitizenship: PGP PR → 1,095 days physical presence in Canada → Citizenship application → Test + Oath → Canadian Citizen
Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).timelineToPR: 3–5 years total: lottery selection (annual, random) + application processing 20–36 months
Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).timelineToCitizenship: 6–8 years from submitting expression of interest
Parent & Grandparent Program (PGP).probabilityNote: The 12% overall probability is dominated by the intake lottery. IRCC receives hundreds of thousands of expressions of interest each year for approximately 15,000–30,000 spots — a selection rate of roughly 5–15% depending on the year and quota. Once selected and an application is submitted, the approval rate is approximately 85–88%. The combined probability (lottery × approval) yields roughly 10–15%. Many sponsors apply for multiple consecutive years before being selected. Note: IRCC also offers the Super Visa as a practical alternative — it allows parents and grandparents to stay in Canada for up to 5 years per entry on a 10-year multiple-entry visa, without granting PR. Source: IRCC PGP intake data, IRCC Annual Report.

Start-up Visa (SUV).description: Canada's Start-up Visa program is designed to attract innovative entrepreneurs by granting PR (not just temporary status) to founders of new Canadian businesses. To qualify, applicants must have a qualifying business idea supported by a designated Canadian organization: a designated venture capital fund (must commit min. $200,000 CAD), a designated angel investor group (must commit min. $75,000 CAD), or a designated business incubator (no minimum investment but competitive selection). The program accepts up to 5 applicants per business. While awaiting PR, applicants receive an open temporary work permit. Language: CLB 5 minimum.
Start-up Visa (SUV).pathToPR: Secure a Letter of Support from a designated VC fund, angel group, or incubator → Apply for open temporary work permit (while PR is processing) → Submit PR application with Letter of Support → IRCC assessment → Background check + medical → Receive PR
Start-up Visa (SUV).pathToCitizenship: SUV PR → 1,095 days physical presence → Citizenship application → Knowledge test + Oath → Canadian Citizen
Start-up Visa (SUV).timelineToPR: 12–36 months (widely variable; IRCC processing times have extended significantly in recent years)
Start-up Visa (SUV).probabilityNote: The 65% probability accounts for two stages: (1) Securing a Letter of Support from a designated organization — highly competitive, with most incubators accepting fewer than 5% of applicants; (2) IRCC's PR approval rate for supported applicants (~90%). The bottleneck is getting the Letter of Support, not the federal PR stage. Additional risk factors include the business failing to meet IRCC's genuineness criteria, insufficient language skills, or the applicant's inability to demonstrate control of the qualifying business (min. 10% voting rights each; combined >50%). The program has faced criticism for processing delays (some applications pending 3+ years), which inflates the effective failure rate. Source: IRCC Start-up Visa program statistics, IRCC processing time data.

AIP Work Permit.description: The Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) is an employer-driven pathway for skilled foreign workers and international graduates who want to settle in Atlantic Canada (New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, or Newfoundland and Labrador). Employers must be designated by their provincial government. The AIP has two streams: (1) Atlantic High-Skilled Program (NOC TEER 0/1/2/3); (2) Atlantic Intermediate-Skilled Program (NOC TEER 4). A settlement plan endorsed by a designated settlement service provider is required. Unique among Canadian programs, the AIP specifically allows intermediate-skilled workers (TEER 4) to access PR — most other federal programs require TEER 0–3.
AIP Work Permit.pathToPR: Find a designated AIP employer in Atlantic Canada → Employer endorses the application → Create a settlement plan with a designated settlement service provider → Submit PR application (no Express Entry required) → IRCC assessment → Background check + medical → Receive PR
AIP Work Permit.pathToCitizenship: AIP PR → 1,095 days physical presence in Atlantic Canada (or anywhere in Canada) → Citizenship application → Knowledge test + Oath → Canadian Citizen
AIP Work Permit.timelineToPR: 6–12 months from employer endorsement to PR
AIP Work Permit.probabilityNote: AIP has a high PR approval rate (~82–85%) because employer endorsement pre-screens candidates before the federal application. Atlantic provinces have strong labor demand and actively promote the program. The main failure points are: (1) The AIP employer losing their designated status or the job falling through before PR is processed; (2) Applicants not meeting the minimum 1-year work experience or language requirements (CLB 4 for Intermediate, CLB 5 for High-Skilled stream). Retention is a separate concern — many AIP PRs eventually migrate to larger Canadian cities (Toronto, Vancouver) after receiving their PR card, which does not invalidate their PR but defeats the program's regional retention intent. Source: IRCC AIP program data, Atlantic provincial government immigration statistics.

Any Canadian PR Pathway.fullName: Canadian Naturalization – Standard 3-Year Physical Presence Rule
Any Canadian PR Pathway.description: Any Permanent Resident of Canada, regardless of which immigration pathway they used to obtain PR, can apply for Canadian citizenship after meeting the physical presence requirement: 1,095 days (3 years) physically present in Canada within the last 5 years. Days spent in Canada as a temporary resident or protected person (student, worker, visitor, refugee claimant) before becoming a PR count as half-days (maximum credit of 365 days / 1 year). Citizenship applicants must also file Canadian income taxes, demonstrate language proficiency (CLB 4), and pass a 20-question citizenship knowledge test on Canada's history, government, and values.
Any Canadian PR Pathway.pathToPR: N/A — you must already be a Permanent Resident to follow this citizenship pathway.
Any Canadian PR Pathway.pathToCitizenship: Hold PR → Accumulate 1,095 days physical presence in Canada within last 5 years → File citizenship application (online) → Biometrics (if not on file) → Citizenship knowledge test (20 questions, 75% pass mark) → Language interview (if applicable) → Oath of Citizenship ceremony → Canadian Citizen
Any Canadian PR Pathway.timelineToCitizenship: 12–24 months from application filing (processing time varies by office; IRCC target is 12 months)
Any Canadian PR Pathway.probabilityNote: IRCC approves approximately 87–90% of citizenship applications filed. The primary reasons for refusal or withdrawal are: (1) Failure to pass the citizenship knowledge test (applicants get one re-test opportunity); (2) Physical presence days falling short on audit; (3) Criminal conviction rendering the applicant prohibited; (4) Tax non-compliance discovered during processing. Approximately 200,000–300,000 new Canadians are naturalized each year. Canada has one of the highest naturalization rates among developed nations — roughly 85% of eligible PRs eventually apply for citizenship, compared to ~65% in the U.S. Source: IRCC Citizenship Program data, Statistics Canada citizenship statistics.

Citizenship by Descent.fullName: Canadian Citizenship by Descent (Born Abroad to a Canadian Parent)
Citizenship by Descent.description: Canadian citizenship is automatically conferred on persons born abroad if at least one parent was a Canadian citizen who was either born in Canada or naturalized in Canada (i.e., not themselves a citizen by descent). This is known as the 'first-generation limit' — citizenship by descent is not automatically passed to the second generation born abroad. If you are the child of a Canadian citizen by descent (a 'second-generation' born abroad), you are NOT automatically a Canadian citizen, though you may be eligible for other immigration pathways. To confirm citizenship by descent, you apply for a Canadian Citizenship Certificate (proof of citizenship).
Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — citizenship by descent does not require PR; you are a citizen from birth if eligible.
Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm parent was a Canadian citizen by birth or naturalization (not by descent) → Gather proof documents (parent's Canadian birth certificate or citizenship certificate, your birth certificate, evidence of parent-child relationship) → Apply for a Canadian Citizenship Certificate → Certificate issued → You are recognized as a Canadian Citizen
Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth; certificate processing takes 12–18 months
Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: For individuals who genuinely meet the eligibility criteria (born abroad with a Canadian-born or naturalized parent), the citizenship certificate approval rate is approximately 94–96%. The main denial reasons are: insufficient documentary evidence of parentage, the parent being a citizen by descent themselves (triggering the first-generation limit), or questions about whether the parent actually had Canadian citizenship at the time of the child's birth. This pathway bypasses the immigration system entirely — no visa, no PR, no CRS score needed. Source: IRCC citizenship certificate processing data, Citizenship Act (R.S.C. 1985, c. C-29).

PGWP.description: The PGWP is an open work permit issued to graduates of eligible Canadian Designated Learning Institutions (DLIs). 'Open' means you can work for any employer in any location — no job offer required to get it. Duration matches the length of your study program, up to a maximum of 3 years (programs of 2+ years get the full 3-year PGWP). It is a one-time permit and cannot be renewed or extended. The PGWP is the critical bridge between graduating on a Study Permit and qualifying for Express Entry CEC, which requires 1 year of skilled Canadian work experience. Spouses of PGWP holders may also receive open work permits.
PGWP.pathToPR: Graduate from eligible DLI → Apply for PGWP within 180 days of graduation → Receive open work permit (1–3 years) → Work 1 year in skilled occupation (NOC TEER 0/1/2/3) → Meet CLB 7 language requirement → Apply to Express Entry CEC → Receive ITA → PR in ~6 months
PGWP.pathToCitizenship: PGWP → PR (CEC) → 1,095 days physical presence → Citizenship test → Oath → Canadian Citizen
PGWP.timelineToPR: 2–4 years from receiving PGWP to PR (1 year work requirement + Express Entry processing)
PGWP.probabilityNote: The 56% probability applies to students who have already graduated and received their PGWP — a significantly higher probability than from the Study Permit stage because these individuals have committed to staying in Canada. PGWP approval rate is approximately 95%+ for eligible graduates. Of those with a valid PGWP, IRCC data suggests approximately 55–60% eventually receive PR, with the main attrition points being: failure to meet CLB 7 language requirement (~15% of graduates), CRS score not reaching ITA cutoff (~10%), and voluntary departure or change of plans (~20%). Graduates in STEM, healthcare, and trades fields have substantially higher success rates due to higher CRS scores and targeted draws. Source: IRCC PGWP data, IRCC Express Entry Year-End Report 2022, Statistics Canada Education Indicators.

permanentResidence.officialName: Australian Permanent Residence Visa
criteria: Meet the points threshold (65+ points) for points-tested streams, or employer/family/business sponsorship criteria
Be under 45 years of age at time of application (for most skilled visas)
Demonstrate English language proficiency (IELTS 6.0+ or equivalent for most streams)
Have qualifications assessed by the relevant Skills Assessment Authority for your nominated occupation
Pass character and health requirements (police clearance, medical examination)
Have your occupation on the relevant skilled occupation list (for skills-based visas)

routes.name: Student Visa (Subclass 500) → Temporary Graduate Visa Pipeline
routes.description: The most common pathway for international students in Australia. After completing a degree at an Australian institution, graduates apply for the Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485) — a post-study open work visa for 2–6 years (no employer or lottery required). During the 485, graduates accumulate skilled work experience and points to enter SkillSelect (Subclass 189 or 190) or secure employer nomination (Subclass 186).
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–12 years: 2–4 years study + 2–6 years Subclass 485 + 1–3 years SkillSelect or employer PR process

routes.name: SkillSelect – Points-Tested Skilled Migration
routes.description: Australia's main points-based system. Candidates submit an Expression of Interest (EOI) and are invited based on points score. Subclass 189 needs no state sponsor (80–90+ points). Subclass 190 requires state nomination (+5 points, typically 75+). Subclass 491 is for regional areas (+15 points, must live regionally for 3 years before applying for Subclass 191 PR).
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–18 months from EOI to PR if points are competitive; 3–5 years via the Subclass 491 regional pathway

routes.name: Employer Sponsored – Subclass 482 → Subclass 186
routes.description: For workers sponsored by an approved Australian employer. Subclass 482 (TSS) is a temporary work visa (2–4 years). Subclass 186 (ENS) leads directly to permanent residence via three pathways: Temporary Residence Transition (after 2 years on 482), Direct Entry, or Labour Agreement.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2–5 years: 2+ years on Subclass 482 + Subclass 186 processing (6–12 months)

routes.name: Family – Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801)
routes.description: For spouses or de facto partners of Australian citizens, permanent residents, or eligible New Zealand citizens. Subclass 820 is the temporary stage; Subclass 801 is permanent, granted approximately 2 years after the initial application (or immediately if the relationship predated the application by 3+ years). Both are applied for simultaneously.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2–3 years total from application to permanent Subclass 801 PR

routes.name: Investor – Business Innovation & Investment (Subclass 188/888)
routes.description: For business owners and investors. Streams: Business Innovation (AUD $750K+ turnover, AUD $1.25M net assets), Investor (AUD $2.5M+), Significant Investor (AUD $5M), Entrepreneur (state-supported). Subclass 188 is provisional (4–5 years); Subclass 888 grants permanent residence after meeting business and investment milestones.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4–8 years: 4–5 years Subclass 188 + 1–2 years Subclass 888 transition

criteria: Hold Australian permanent residence
Have lived in Australia for 4 years immediately before applying, including at least 12 months as a permanent resident
Not have been absent from Australia for more than 12 months in the 4 years (no more than 90 days in the final 12-month PR period)
Be of good character (no serious criminal history)
Pass the Australian citizenship test (20 questions on values, history, and government; 75% pass mark required)
Be at least 18 years old (minors can be included in a parent's application)

routes.name: Naturalization – General (4-Year Rule)
routes.description: The standard path to Australian citizenship. After 4 years of lawful residence in Australia (including 12 months as a PR), applicants take the citizenship test and attend a ceremony to take the Australian Citizenship Pledge.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4 years as a resident (1 year as PR) + 12–24 months citizenship application processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent
routes.description: A person born outside Australia may be an Australian citizen by descent if at least one parent was an Australian citizen at the time of the person's birth. Citizenship must be claimed through registration — it is not automatic.
routes.estimatedDuration: Immediate eligibility from birth; registration processing 6–18 months

Student Visa (Subclass 500).description: The Subclass 500 student visa allows full-time study at a CRICOS-registered Australian institution. It does not directly lead to PR but starts a well-structured pipeline: graduate → Temporary Graduate Visa (485, open work, no lottery) → accumulate skills assessment + IELTS + work experience → SkillSelect (Subclass 189/190) or employer nomination (Subclass 186). Australia's route is significantly more accessible than the U.S. F-1 path because there is no H-1B-style lottery and post-study work rights are granted automatically to eligible graduates.
Student Visa (Subclass 500).pathToPR: Subclass 500 Student Visa → Graduate from CRICOS institution → Apply for Subclass 485 (Temporary Graduate Visa) → Work in Australia on open work rights → Complete skills assessment + achieve IELTS 6.0+ → Submit EOI in SkillSelect → Receive ITA for Subclass 189 or 190 (or secure employer sponsorship for Subclass 186) → Receive PR
Student Visa (Subclass 500).pathToCitizenship: Subclass 500 Student → Subclass 485 Post-Study Work → PR (189/190/186) → Be lawfully present in Australia for 4 years total (student + work time all counts) with the last 12 months as a permanent resident → Pass the citizenship test → Attend citizenship ceremony → Australian Citizen
Student Visa (Subclass 500).timelineToPR: 6–12 years from starting studies: 2–4 years degree + 2–6 years on Subclass 485 + 1–3 years SkillSelect or employer process
Student Visa (Subclass 500).timelineToCitizenship: 7–11 years from starting studies: 3–4 years study + 2–4 years post-study work → PR (6–18 months processing) → 1 more year as PR → citizenship (4-year total Australia presence with last 12 months as PR — student time counts toward the 4 years)
Student Visa (Subclass 500).probabilityNote: Australia's citizenship path is more accessible than the data previously indicated. The 4-year total Australia presence requirement (with only the last 12 months requiring PR status) means student time fully counts. A student who studies 3 years, works 2 years, gets PR, and stays 1 more year is eligible in ~6–7 years. The 38% probability reflects: selective skilled migration visa requirements (many students can't meet points thresholds or employer sponsorship), PR processing delays, and the segment who return home after studies. Australia permits dual citizenship. Source: Australian Department of Home Affairs statistics.

Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485).fullName: Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485) – Post-Study Work Stream
Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485).description: The Subclass 485 is Australia's post-study work visa — the critical bridge between graduating and obtaining PR. It grants open work rights (any employer, any location) for 2 years (bachelor's or coursework master's), 3 years (research master's or doctoral), or 4–6 years (degrees in regional areas, as of 2023 reforms). No employer sponsorship or points score required — just proof of graduation from an eligible CRICOS-registered institution.
Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485).pathToPR: Receive Subclass 485 → Work full-time in a skilled occupation → Complete skills assessment (e.g. Engineers Australia, ACS) → Achieve IELTS 6.0+ → Submit EOI in SkillSelect → Receive Invitation to Apply (ITA) when points score is competitive → Apply for Subclass 189 or 190 (or 482 → 186 employer route) → Receive PR
Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485).pathToCitizenship: Subclass 485 → PR (189/190/186) → 4 years residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485).timelineToPR: 2–6 years from receiving Subclass 485 to PR (depending on occupation demand, state nomination availability, and points score)
Temporary Graduate Visa (Subclass 485).probabilityNote: Probability is calculated from the Subclass 485 stage (post-graduation, committed to working in Australia). Subclass 485 approval rate is approximately 95%+ for eligible graduates. Of 485 holders who pursue skilled work, approximately 50–55% eventually receive PR. Main attrition points: failing to meet the 65-point SkillSelect threshold, occupation removed from the skilled list, skills assessment rejection, or departure before completing the PR pathway.
