Regional graduates have higher PR rates due to bonus points from regional study and work. Source: Department of Home Affairs visa statistics, Parliamentary Budget Office reports.

Skilled Independent Visa (Subclass 189).description: Subclass 189 is a permanent residence visa for skilled workers requiring no state or employer sponsorship — the most portable Australian PR pathway. Points are scored on age (max 30), English (max 20), skilled employment (max 20), educational qualifications (max 20), and bonus factors. In competitive occupations (IT, engineering, accounting), the minimum cutoff in invitation rounds is often 80–90+ points. Applicants must be under 45 at time of invitation.
Skilled Independent Visa (Subclass 189).pathToPR: Get skills assessed by relevant authority → Take IELTS/PTE (6.0+ minimum) → Submit EOI in SkillSelect → Receive ITA when score meets invitation round cutoff → Submit full PR application within 60 days → Medical exam + police clearance → Receive Subclass 189 PR
Skilled Independent Visa (Subclass 189).pathToCitizenship: Subclass 189 PR → 4 years Australian residence (1 year as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Skilled Independent Visa (Subclass 189).timelineToPR: 6–18 months from EOI to PR if points score is competitive (80–90+ for most professions); may take years if score is borderline
Skilled Independent Visa (Subclass 189).probabilityNote: Subclass 189 application approval rate is approximately 85–90% for those who receive an ITA — the points score pre-screens applicants. However, only about 70–75% of EOI candidates receive an ITA within 2 years (many scores are never competitive enough). The 68% reflects the combined probability from EOI submission. Major risk factors: skills assessment rejection (~10%), age-out (over 45), or occupation removed from the skilled list. Source: Department of Home Affairs Subclass 189 statistics, SkillSelect quarterly reports.

Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190).fullName: Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190) – State/Territory Nominated PR
Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190).description: Subclass 190 is a permanent residence visa for skilled workers nominated by an Australian state or territory. State nomination adds 5 points to the SkillSelect score, lowering the competitive threshold. In exchange, applicants commit to living and working in the nominating state or territory for 2 years. Each state has its own nomination criteria based on local labour market needs — some specifically target occupations not in federal demand.
Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190).pathToPR: Identify a state with an open stream for your occupation → Apply for state nomination → Receive nomination certificate → Submit EOI in SkillSelect (+5 points) → Receive ITA → Submit PR application → Medical + police check → Receive Subclass 190 PR
Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190).pathToCitizenship: Subclass 190 PR → 4 years residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190).timelineToPR: 6–18 months from state nomination to receiving PR
Skilled Nominated Visa (Subclass 190).probabilityNote: Subclass 190 has a slightly higher overall probability than Subclass 189 because state nomination pre-screens candidates based on local demand. State nomination approval rates vary by stream: job-offer streams ~85%, human capital streams ~70–75%. Once nominated and ITA received, federal PR approval is ~90%. The 74% accounts for state nomination success rates and federal processing. Source: Department of Home Affairs visa statistics, state government immigration data.

Skilled Work Regional Visa (Subclass 491.description: Subclass 491 is a 5-year provisional visa for skilled workers willing to live and work in regional Australia. State/territory or family sponsorship required. Adds 15 bonus SkillSelect points (making it far more accessible than 189/190). After 3 years living/working in a regional area and meeting income thresholds (~AUD $53,900/year), holders apply for Subclass 191 Permanent Residence. Regional areas cover most of Australia outside Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Gold Coast, and Perth.
Skilled Work Regional Visa (Subclass 491.pathToPR: Get skills assessed → Apply for state/territory or family sponsorship → Receive ITA in SkillSelect (+15 regional points) → Receive Subclass 491 (5-year provisional) → Live and work in regional area for 3 years → Meet income threshold → Apply for Subclass 191 Permanent Residence → Receive PR
Skilled Work Regional Visa (Subclass 491.pathToCitizenship: Subclass 491 → Subclass 191 PR → 4 years residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship → Australian Citizen
Skilled Work Regional Visa (Subclass 491.timelineToPR: 3–5 years from receiving Subclass 491 to PR (3 years regional residence + Subclass 191 processing)
Skilled Work Regional Visa (Subclass 491.probabilityNote: Subclass 491 approval rate is high (~85–90%) due to the lower points threshold and regional demand. However, Subclass 191 PR conversion is approximately 75–80% of 491 holders who complete 3 years in the regional area — main attrition is applicants who move to metro areas before meeting requirements or fail the income threshold. The 65% reflects the full pipeline from 491 application to PR. Source: Department of Home Affairs regional visa statistics, regional settlement data.

Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass.fullName: Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass 482) – Employer Sponsored Work Visa
Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass.description: Subclass 482 (formerly the 457 visa) is a temporary work visa allowing approved Australian employers to sponsor skilled foreign workers for up to 4 years. Employers must be approved sponsors and pay at least the Temporary Skilled Migration Income Threshold (TSMIT, currently AUD $70,000/year). Subclass 482 is the main stepping stone to permanent Subclass 186 via the Temporary Residence Transition (TRT) pathway after 2+ years of employment with the same employer.
Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass.pathToPR: Employer obtains Standard Business Sponsorship → Employer nominates position → Applicant applies for Subclass 482 → Work for the same employer for 2+ years → Employer nominates for Subclass 186 (TRT pathway) → Receive permanent Subclass 186 PR
Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass.pathToCitizenship: Subclass 482 → Subclass 186 PR → 4 years Australian residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass.timelineToPR: 2–4 years on Subclass 482 + 6–12 months for Subclass 186 processing = 3–5 years total
Temporary Skill Shortage Visa (Subclass.probabilityNote: Of Subclass 482 holders who work in Australia for 2+ years, approximately 55–60% eventually transition to PR via Subclass 186 TRT or another pathway. Main attrition factors: employer withdrawing sponsorship, employer losing approved sponsor status, change of occupation outside the relevant list, or the worker choosing to leave Australia. The Subclass 186 TRT approval rate is ~85% for eligible applicants. Source: Department of Home Affairs employer sponsorship statistics.

Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186.fullName: Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186) – Direct Permanent Residence
Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186.description: Subclass 186 is a permanent residence visa sponsored by an approved Australian employer. Three pathways: (1) Temporary Residence Transition (TRT) — for those who worked 2+ years with the same 482 sponsor employer; (2) Direct Entry — for skilled workers with a job offer and skills assessment; (3) Labour Agreement — for employers with a sector-specific government agreement. TRT has less stringent skills assessment requirements than Direct Entry.
Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186.pathToPR: Employer obtains Standard Business Sponsorship → Employer nominates position → Applicant applies for Subclass 186 → Medical exam + police clearance → Receive permanent PR (Subclass 186)
Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186.pathToCitizenship: Subclass 186 PR → 4 years Australian residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186.timelineToPR: 6–18 months from application to PR (Direct Entry); 6–12 months (TRT)
Employer Nomination Scheme (Subclass 186.probabilityNote: Subclass 186 approval rate is approximately 80–85% overall — TRT pathway has slightly higher rates (~88%) since candidates have pre-established employment records. Direct Entry has lower rates (~78%) due to skills assessment scrutiny. Main denial reasons: occupation not on the relevant skilled list, skills assessment rejection, employer not genuinely needing the worker, or health/character failures. Source: Department of Home Affairs employer visa statistics.

Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801).fullName: Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801) – Spouse or De Facto Partner
Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801).description: For spouses or de facto partners (including same-sex) of Australian citizens, permanent residents, or eligible New Zealand citizens. Subclass 820 is the temporary stage; Subclass 801 is the permanent stage — both applied for simultaneously with one application. The temporary 820 is typically granted within 12–18 months. The permanent 801 is decided 2 years after the 820 application date (or sooner if the relationship has been ongoing for 3+ years at application). Applicants must prove a genuine and ongoing relationship.
Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801).pathToPR: Apply for combined Subclass 820/801 → Provide evidence of genuine relationship (joint finances, cohabitation, social recognition) → Medical exam + character check → Receive Subclass 820 (temporary) → Department assesses relationship at 2-year mark → Receive Subclass 801 (permanent PR)
Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801).pathToCitizenship: Subclass 801 PR → 4 years Australian residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801).timelineToPR: 2–3 years total from application to Subclass 801 permanent PR
Partner Visa (Subclass 820/801).probabilityNote: Partner visa approval rates are approximately 86–90%. The primary denial reason is inability to prove a genuine relationship (most refusals). Same-sex couples and de facto couples may face additional scrutiny. Once the Subclass 820 temporary visa is granted, the transition to permanent 801 is approved in ~95% of cases where the relationship continues. Source: Department of Home Affairs partner visa statistics.

Business Innovation and Investment Visa.description: A pathway for business owners, investors, and entrepreneurs. Subclass 188 streams: Business Innovation (AUD $750K+ turnover, net assets AUD $1.25M+), Investor (AUD $2.5M+ in state bonds), Significant Investor (AUD $5M complying investment), Entrepreneur (state endorsement required). Subclass 188 is a 4-year provisional visa; after meeting business/investment milestones, holders apply for Subclass 888 (permanent PR). State/territory nomination is required for all streams.
Business Innovation and Investment Visa.pathToPR: Receive state nomination → Apply for Subclass 188 provisional → Establish business or make investment → Operate for required period → Meet financial and business criteria → Apply for Subclass 888 permanent PR → Receive PR
Business Innovation and Investment Visa.pathToCitizenship: Subclass 888 PR → 4 years residence (1 as PR) → Citizenship test → Pledge → Australian Citizen
Business Innovation and Investment Visa.timelineToPR: 4–8 years from initial Subclass 188 to Subclass 888 PR
Business Innovation and Investment Visa.probabilityNote: Subclass 188 initial approval rate is ~75–80%, but Subclass 888 transition is only ~65–70% of 188 holders — many fail to meet business/investment performance criteria during the provisional period. The combined end-to-end probability is approximately 50–55%. Significant Investor stream (AUD $5M) has the highest transition rate due to complying investment funds. Source: Department of Home Affairs business visa statistics, KPMG Australian Business Migration Report.

Any Australian Permanent Residence Visa.description: Any holder of an Australian permanent residence visa can apply for citizenship after 4 years of lawful residence in Australia, with at least 12 months as a permanent resident. Absences are allowed up to 12 months total (no more than 90 days in the final 12 months). Applicants must be of good character, pass the citizenship test (75% pass mark, 20 questions on Australian values, history, and government), and attend a citizenship ceremony.
Any Australian Permanent Residence Visa.pathToPR: N/A — you must already hold Australian PR to follow this pathway.
Any Australian Permanent Residence Visa.pathToCitizenship: Hold Australian PR → Accumulate 4 years lawful residence (1 year as PR) → Apply for citizenship → Pass citizenship test → Attend citizenship ceremony → Take Australian Citizenship Pledge → Australian Citizen
Any Australian Permanent Residence Visa.probabilityNote: The Australian citizenship test has a pass rate of approximately 98% (considered straightforward). The overall citizenship application approval rate is approximately 85–88% — main refusal reasons are character grounds (serious criminal history), failure to meet the physical presence requirement, or absences exceeding allowed limits. Approximately 140,000–200,000 people become Australian citizens annually through naturalization. Source: Department of Home Affairs Citizenship Program statistics, Australian National Audit Office reports.

Australian Citizenship by Descent.description: A person born outside Australia may be an Australian citizen by descent if at least one parent was an Australian citizen at the time of the person's birth. Unlike many countries, Australia allows multi-generational citizenship by descent as long as each generation's parent was an Australian citizen at the time of the child's birth — there is no strict first-generation limit. However, citizenship must be claimed through registration — it is not automatic and does not appear on standard documents without registration.
Australian Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — citizenship by descent bypasses PR entirely.
Australian Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm parent was an Australian citizen at time of your birth → Gather documents (parent's Australian passport/citizenship certificate, your birth certificate, evidence of relationship) → Apply for citizenship by descent registration with Department of Home Affairs → Receive citizenship certificate → Australian Citizen
Australian Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth; registration processing 6–18 months
Australian Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: Citizenship by descent registration approval rate is approximately 90–93% for those who genuinely meet the eligibility criteria. Primary denial reasons: insufficient documentary evidence of the parent's Australian citizenship at time of birth, parentage disputes, or questions about whether the claimed parent-child relationship is genuine. Source: Department of Home Affairs citizenship by descent program statistics.

permanentResidence.officialName: New Zealand Residence Class Visa
criteria: Have a job offer or current employment in a skilled role in New Zealand (for most pathways)
Meet health and character requirements (medical examination and police clearance)
Demonstrate English language proficiency appropriate to the visa category
Have relevant qualifications or work experience recognised in New Zealand
Meet minimum income or investment thresholds where applicable

routes.name: Student Visa → Post-Study Work Visa Pipeline
routes.description: The standard pathway for international students. After completing a New Zealand degree (level 7+), graduates apply for a Post-Study Work Visa (PSWV) — up to 3 years of open work with no employer or lottery required. During the PSWV, graduates accumulate skilled work experience to qualify for the Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident Visa or Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV). No H-1B-style lottery is involved.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–10 years: 2–4 years study + 1–3 years Post-Study Work Visa + 6–18 months SMC processing

routes.name: Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident Visa
routes.description: New Zealand's primary points-based permanent residence pathway. Points are awarded for employment in NZ (especially skilled or shortage occupations), qualifications, work experience, job offer, and bonus factors (NZ partner, NZ family, study in NZ). Those above 160 points are automatically selected; lower scores enter fortnightly draws based on demand. Must be under 56 at time of EOI submission.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–18 months from Expression of Interest to receiving resident visa

routes.name: Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV) → Residence
routes.description: The main employer-sponsored work visa in New Zealand (introduced 2022, replacing Essential Skills visa). Employers must be accredited with Immigration NZ. Granted for up to 5 years. After 24 months of skilled work at or above the median wage (NZD $29.66/hour), AEWV holders can apply for SMC Resident Visa. Some shortage occupations have fast-tracked residence pathways.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2–4 years on AEWV + 6–18 months SMC/residence processing = 3–6 years total

routes.name: Family – Partnership Resident Visa
routes.description: For genuine partners (married, civil union, or de facto for 12+ months) of New Zealand citizens or resident visa holders. New Zealand grants a 2-year open work visa first, then a Resident Visa after 2 years of living with the NZ partner (or directly if the relationship is 3+ years established). Same-sex couples are fully eligible.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2–3 years from initial application to Resident Visa

routes.name: Investor Resident Visa
routes.description: Two streams: Investor (NZD $3M+ invested for 3 years, under 66 years old, must spend 88 days in NZ in last 2 years of investment period) and Investor Plus (NZD $10M+ for 3 years, no age limit, only 21 days in-country required). Both grant Resident Visas upon completing the investment period.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3–4 years from initial application to formal resident visa status

criteria: Hold a New Zealand Resident Visa
Have been present in New Zealand for at least 1,350 days in the 5 years immediately before applying
Have been present in New Zealand for at least 240 days in each of those 5 years
Be of good character (no serious criminal history)
Intend to continue living in New Zealand or maintain an association with it
Be at least 16 years old (children can be included in a parent's application)

routes.name: Naturalization after Permanent Residence (5-Year Rule)
routes.description: The standard path to New Zealand citizenship. After 5 years of presence in New Zealand as a resident (minimum 240 days per year, 1,350 days total), applicants apply for citizenship by grant. Includes a character assessment, oath or affirmation of allegiance, and a formal citizenship ceremony.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years of qualifying presence + 12–18 months processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born to New Zealand Citizen Parent)
routes.description: A person born outside New Zealand on or after 1 January 2006 is a citizen by descent if at least one parent was a New Zealand citizen (by birth, grant, or descent) at the time of birth. First-generation limit applies since 2006 — grandchildren of NZ citizens born abroad are not automatic citizens if their parent was also only a citizen by descent born outside NZ. Must register to use this citizenship.
routes.estimatedDuration: Citizenship exists from birth; registration 6–12 months

Student Visa (New Zealand).fullName: New Zealand Student Visa → Permanent Residence Pipeline
Student Visa (New Zealand).description: The New Zealand Student Visa allows international students to study full-time at an eligible institution. By itself it does not lead to PR, but it starts a clear pipeline: graduate → Post-Study Work Visa (open work, no employer needed) → gain skilled work experience → apply for SMC Resident Visa or AEWV employer sponsorship. NZ's student-to-PR path has no lottery and is significantly faster than the U.S. F-1 route, though points thresholds require skilled NZ work experience.
Student Visa (New Zealand).pathToPR: Student Visa → Study at NZ institution → Graduate → Apply for Post-Study Work Visa (PSWV, up to 3 years open work) → Gain 12–24 months skilled NZ work experience → Meet SMC 160-point threshold or secure AEWV → Submit EOI → Receive ITA → Apply for SMC Resident Visa → Receive PR
Student Visa (New Zealand).pathToCitizenship: Student Visa → PSWV → Skilled Migrant Resident Visa (PR) → Be present in New Zealand for at least 1,350 days (3.7 years) in the 5 years before applying — student + work + PR time all count → Have been a permanent resident for at least the 12 months immediately before applying → Citizenship grant → New Zealand Citizen
Student Visa (New Zealand).timelineToPR: 6–10 years from starting studies: 2–4 years degree + 1–3 years PSWV + 6–18 months SMC processing
Student Visa (New Zealand).timelineToCitizenship: 6–10 years from starting studies: 3–4 years study (counts toward 1,350-day presence) + 1–3 years work → PR → 1 more year as PR → citizenship (student time fully counts toward the 5-year/1,350-day presence requirement)
Student Visa (New Zealand).probabilityNote: New Zealand's citizenship path is more accessible than previously stated. The 1,350-day (3.7-year) presence requirement within 5 years counts student, work, and permanent resident time — a student who studies 3 years and works 2 years on a work visa has already met the presence requirement before even getting PR. Only 1 year of PR is required before applying. The 35% probability reflects: SMC points threshold difficulty (many students can't accumulate enough skilled migrant points), processing delays, and return-home decisions. NZ permits dual citizenship. Source: NZ Department of Internal Affairs citizenship statistics.

Post-Study Work Visa (New Zealand).fullName: Post-Study Work Visa (PSWV) – New Zealand Open Work Permit
Post-Study Work Visa (New Zealand).description: The PSWV is New Zealand's post-study open work visa, allowing graduates of eligible NZ qualifications to live and work for any employer for up to 3 years. Duration: 1 year (NZ Diploma Level 5–6), 2 years (Level 7 bachelor's at a polytechnic), 3 years (Level 7 bachelor's at a university, Level 8–10 postgraduate). The PSWV is the critical bridge to building skilled work experience in NZ, which is heavily weighted in the SMC points system.
Post-Study Work Visa (New Zealand).pathToPR: Graduate from eligible NZ qualification → Apply for PSWV (within 3 months of graduation) → Work in NZ → Accumulate 12–24 months skilled NZ work experience → Meet SMC 160-point threshold → Submit EOI → Receive ITA → Apply for SMC Resident Visa → Receive PR
Post-Study Work Visa (New Zealand).pathToCitizenship: PSWV → SMC Resident Visa → 5 years NZ presence → Citizenship by grant → New Zealand Citizen
Post-Study Work Visa (New Zealand).timelineToPR: 1–4 years from receiving PSWV to PR (1 year minimum work + SMC processing)
Post-Study Work Visa (New Zealand).probabilityNote: Of PSWV holders who remain in NZ and gain skilled employment, approximately 45–50% eventually obtain PR. Main attrition: failure to accumulate skilled (not just any) NZ work experience, insufficient SMC points (especially for Level 5–6 diploma holders), or voluntary departure. University graduates have considerably higher success rates (~60%) than polytechnic diploma graduates (~30%) due to higher education points in SMC. Source: Immigration NZ PSWV outcome data, MBIE immigration longitudinal studies.

Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident.fullName: Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident Visa – Points-Based Permanent Residence
Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident.description: New Zealand's main points-based permanent residence pathway. Points awarded for: current NZ employment (50 base + bonuses for skilled/shortage occupations), qualifications (up to 70 points), work experience (up to 30 points), and bonus factors (partner's qualifications, NZ siblings, study in NZ). Minimum 160 points for automatic pool selection; lower scores enter fortnightly draws. Applicants must be under 56 years of age at EOI submission.
Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident.pathToPR: Assess eligibility and points score → Submit EOI (Expression of Interest) → Selected from pool (automatic if 160+ points, draw if below) → Invited to apply for SMC Resident Visa → Submit full application with evidence → Health and character checks → Receive Resident Visa (permanent PR)
Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident.pathToCitizenship: SMC Resident Visa → 5 years NZ presence (1,350 days, 240+/year) → Apply for citizenship → Oath → New Zealand Citizen
Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident.timelineToPR: 6–18 months from EOI submission (faster if 160+ points; slower if waiting for draw selection)
Skilled Migrant Category (SMC) Resident.probabilityNote: SMC Resident Visa approval rate is approximately 85–90% for those selected from the pool and invited to apply, since the points system pre-screens candidates. However, only about 75–80% of EOI candidates receive an invitation within 2 years — those with scores between 100–160 may wait many months. The 70% reflects the full pipeline from EOI to resident visa. Source: Immigration New Zealand SMC statistics, MBIE immigration data.

Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV).fullName: Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV) – Employer-Sponsored Pathway to Residence
Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV).description: Introduced in 2022, the AEWV is the primary employer-sponsored work visa in New Zealand. Employers must be accredited with Immigration NZ. Minimum pay must be at or above the median wage (NZD $29.66/hour in 2024). Granted for up to 5 years. AEWV holders can apply for PR via SMC after 24 months of employment at the required salary. Holders in certain shortage occupations (teachers, nurses, engineers) may be eligible for fast-tracked residence pathways.
Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV).pathToPR: Secure job with accredited employer → Employer checks role → Apply for AEWV → Work for 24 months at or above median wage → Meet SMC points (NZ employment earns 50 base + occupation bonus) → Submit SMC EOI → Receive ITA → Apply for SMC Resident Visa → Receive PR
Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV).pathToCitizenship: AEWV → SMC Resident Visa → 5 years NZ presence → Citizenship → New Zealand Citizen
Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV).timelineToPR: 2–4 years on AEWV before SMC eligibility + 6–18 months SMC processing = 3–6 years total
Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV).probabilityNote: AEWV holders who gain 24 months of employment and meet SMC criteria have approximately 60–65% chance of obtaining PR. However, the overall probability from obtaining an AEWV is lower (~48%) because many holders leave before reaching 24 months, work below the median wage threshold, change employers, or fail to reach the SMC points threshold. Source: Immigration NZ AEWV statistics, MBIE data.

Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zea.fullName: Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zealand) – Spouse or De Facto Partner
Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zea.description: For genuine partners (married, civil union, or de facto for 12+ months) of New Zealand citizens or resident visa holders. NZ initially grants a 2-year interim open work visa, then the Resident Visa after confirming the relationship is genuine and ongoing. Partners who have been together for 3+ years may receive the Resident Visa directly. Same-sex couples have been fully included since NZ legalised same-sex marriage in 2013. Both partners must provide extensive evidence of their genuine relationship.
Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zea.pathToPR: NZ citizen/resident partner submits support → Apply for 2-year interim Partnership Work Visa (or directly Resident Visa if 3+ years together) → Live with NZ partner → After 2 years, apply for Partnership Resident Visa → Demonstrate genuine ongoing relationship → Receive PR
Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zea.pathToCitizenship: Partnership Resident Visa → 5 years NZ presence (1,350 days) → Citizenship by grant → New Zealand Citizen
Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zea.timelineToPR: 2–3 years from initial application to Resident Visa
Partnership-Based Resident Visa (New Zea.probabilityNote: Partnership visa applications have an approval rate of approximately 84–88%. Refusal is primarily due to inability to prove a genuine relationship — Immigration NZ scrutinizes evidence carefully. The interim stage has a high conversion rate to permanent residence (~93%) where the relationship continues. Same-sex couples and de facto couples face no discrimination under NZ's Human Rights Act. Source: Immigration New Zealand partnership visa statistics.

Investor Visa (New Zealand).fullName: Investor Resident Visa (New Zealand) – Residence via Investment
Investor Visa (New Zealand).description: Two investor streams: Investor (NZD $3 million invested for 3 years, under 66 years old, English proficiency, 88 days presence in last 2 years) and Investor Plus (NZD $10 million for 3 years, no age limit, only 21 days in-country required). Eligible investments include NZ bonds, NZ company shares, and approved managed funds. Both streams grant a Resident Visa upon entering New Zealand and completing the investment period.
Investor Visa (New Zealand).pathToPR: Submit EOI → Receive invitation to apply → Complete character/health checks → Submit investment funds → Enter NZ → Hold complying investment for 3 years → Meet presence requirements → Receive formal Resident Visa confirmation
Investor Visa (New Zealand).pathToCitizenship: Investor Resident Visa → 5 years NZ presence (challenging for Investor Plus — may need additional years beyond the investment period) → Citizenship by grant → New Zealand Citizen
Investor Visa (New Zealand).timelineToPR: 3–4 years from application to formal PR status
Investor Visa (New Zealand).probabilityNote: Investor Resident Visa approval rate is approximately 75–82% for those who formally apply — main denial reasons are character issues, source-of-funds questions, and failure to meet investment minimums. The 3-year holding period creates additional risk — early liquidation of the investment can result in visa cancellation. Investor Plus has slightly higher success rates due to the larger investment demonstrating serious commitment. Source: Immigration New Zealand investor visa statistics.

Any New Zealand Resident Visa.fullName: New Zealand Citizenship by Grant – Standard 5-Year Rule
Any New Zealand Resident Visa.description: Any holder of a New Zealand Resident Visa can apply for citizenship by grant after meeting the physical presence requirement: at least 1,350 days in New Zealand in the 5 years immediately before applying, with at least 240 days in each of those 5 years. Applicants must be of good character, demonstrate their identity, and take an oath or affirmation of allegiance at a citizenship ceremony.
Any New Zealand Resident Visa.pathToPR: N/A — you must already hold NZ Resident Visa status to follow this pathway.
Any New Zealand Resident Visa.pathToCitizenship: Hold NZ Resident Visa → Be present in NZ for 1,350 days in the last 5 years (240+ days/year) → Apply for citizenship by grant → Character/identity check → Attend citizenship ceremony → Take oath of allegiance → New Zealand Citizen
Any New Zealand Resident Visa.probabilityNote: New Zealand citizenship applications have an approval rate of approximately 86–90%. Most common refusal reasons: failure to meet the physical presence requirement (the 240-day-per-year requirement is strict), character issues (undisclosed criminal history), or identity document discrepancies. Approximately 95% of eligible PRs who apply are granted citizenship. Source: Department of Internal Affairs citizenship statistics.

New Zealand Citizenship by Descent.description: Persons born outside New Zealand on or after 1 January 2006 are citizens by descent if at least one parent was a New Zealand citizen (by birth, grant, or descent) at the time of the child's birth. The first-generation limit applies since 2006 — if the parent was only a citizen by descent and was born outside NZ, citizenship does not automatically pass to the next generation. The child must be registered to use this citizenship — it does not appear on standard documents automatically.
New Zealand Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — citizenship by descent bypasses PR entirely.
New Zealand Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm parent was a NZ citizen (by birth or grant, or by descent if born before 2006 rules applied) at time of your birth → Gather documents (parent's NZ passport or citizenship certificate, your birth certificate, evidence of relationship) → Apply for citizenship by descent registration → Receive NZ citizenship certificate → New Zealand Citizen
New Zealand Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth; registration 6–12 months
New Zealand Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: Citizenship by descent registration has an approval rate of ~91–94% for those who genuinely meet eligibility criteria. Denial is primarily due to: parent was a NZ citizen by descent born outside NZ (triggering the first-generation limit post-2006), insufficient documentary evidence of the parent's NZ citizenship at time of birth, or parentage disputes. Source: Department of Internal Affairs citizenship by descent data.

permanentResidence.officialName: Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR)
criteria: Have spent the required continuous period in the UK on a qualifying visa (typically 5 years)
Not have exceeded 180 days outside the UK in any 12-month period during the qualifying period
Meet the English language requirement (B1 CEFR level for most routes)
Pass the Life in the UK Test (24 questions, 75% pass mark)
Have no serious criminal record or outstanding immigration violations
Pay the ILR application fee and provide biometrics

routes.name: Student Visa → Graduate Route → ILR Pipeline
routes.description: The main pathway for international students in the UK. After graduating from a UK degree, students apply for the Graduate Route — a 2-year (3-year for PhD) open work visa with no employer sponsorship or lottery required. After the Graduate Route, workers switch to a Skilled Worker Visa with an employer sponsor. After 5 cumulative years on qualifying visas (including Graduate Route time), applicants can apply for ILR.
routes.estimatedDuration: 8–12 years: 3–4 years study + 2–3 years Graduate Route + remaining years on Skilled Worker Visa to reach 5-year ILR qualifying period

routes.name: Skilled Worker Visa → Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR)
routes.description: The primary route for employer-sponsored skilled workers. Requires a job offer from a UK employer with a Home Office Sponsorship Licence, at a salary meeting the general threshold (£38,700 from April 2024). The Global Talent Visa (for leaders in science, humanities, engineering, arts, or digital tech) is endorsement-based with no salary threshold, leading to ILR in 3 years (Exceptional Talent) or 5 years (Exceptional Promise).
routes.estimatedDuration: 5–6 years on qualifying visas + 3–6 months ILR processing

routes.name: Family Visa → ILR
routes.description: For spouses, civil partners, and unmarried partners of British citizens or people settled in the UK. Must meet the financial requirement (sponsor earns £29,000+/year from April 2024). Granted for 30 months, then extended for another 30 months (total 5 years) before ILR. Spouses of British citizens can apply for naturalisation after holding ILR — or use the faster 3-year spouse naturalisation route.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years (two 30-month stages) to ILR + 12–18 months naturalisation after ILR

routes.name: UK Ancestry Visa → ILR
routes.description: For Commonwealth nationals (Australian, Canadian, Indian, South African, etc.) who have a grandparent born in the UK. Allows 5 years of open work in the UK with no employer sponsorship or salary threshold required. After 5 years continuous residence, applicants can apply for ILR. One of the most accessible routes for eligible Commonwealth nationals.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years on UK Ancestry Visa + 3–6 months ILR processing

criteria: Hold Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR) or Settled Status
Have been in the UK for 5 years before applying (3 years if spouse of a British citizen)
Not have been outside the UK for more than 450 days in the 5 years before applying (270 days for the 3-year spouse route)
Not have been outside the UK for more than 90 days in the 12 months before applying
Be of good character (no serious criminal record, no immigration violations in the last 10 years)
Meet the English language requirement (B1 CEFR, typically shown via the Life in the UK test)
Pass the Life in the UK Test (24 questions, 75% pass mark)
Be at least 18 years old

routes.name: Naturalization – General (5-Year Rule)
routes.description: The standard path to British citizenship. After holding ILR for 12 months and having spent 5 years in the UK (no more than 450 days absent), applicants apply for naturalisation via Form AN. Must pass the Life in the UK Test and meet the English language requirement. A naturalisation ceremony follows approval.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years in UK + 12 months ILR hold + 3–6 months naturalisation processing

routes.name: Naturalization via Marriage to British Citizen (3-Year Rule)
routes.description: Spouses or civil partners of British citizens can apply for naturalisation after only 3 years in the UK, with a reduced absence allowance of 270 days total (90 days in the final year). Must hold ILR at time of application. This is the fastest standard path to British citizenship for foreign nationals.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3 years in UK + 12 months ILR hold + 3–6 months naturalisation processing

routes.name: British Citizenship by Descent
routes.description: A person born outside the UK to a parent who was a British citizen 'otherwise than by descent' (born in the UK, or naturalised in the UK) is automatically a British citizen by descent. The first-generation limit applies — British citizenship by descent cannot be automatically passed to the next generation born outside the UK.
routes.estimatedDuration: Automatic from birth; passport/registration processing 3–12 months

Student Visa (UK).fullName: UK Student Visa → Permanent Residence Pipeline
Student Visa (UK).description: The UK Student Visa allows international students to study at a licensed UK education provider. It is the entry point for the most common student-to-settlement pipeline: Student Visa → Graduate Route (2–3 year open work, no lottery) → Skilled Worker Visa (employer-sponsored) → ILR after 5 qualifying years → British Citizenship. Unlike the U.S., there is no H-1B lottery. The main challenges are meeting the high salary threshold for the Skilled Worker Visa (£38,700+ from April 2024) and maintaining continuous qualifying residence for 5 years.
Student Visa (UK).pathToPR: Student Visa → Graduate from UK institution → Apply for Graduate Route (2 years open work, 3 years for PhD) → Find employer with Sponsor Licence → Switch to Skilled Worker Visa (earn £38,700+/year) → Complete 5-year continuous qualifying period → Pass Life in the UK Test → Apply for ILR → Receive ILR
Student Visa (UK).pathToCitizenship: Student Visa → Graduate Route → Skilled Worker → ILR → Hold ILR 12 months → Apply for naturalisation → Life in the UK test → Ceremony → British Citizen
Student Visa (UK).timelineToPR: 8–12 years from starting studies: 3–4 years study + 2–3 years Graduate Route + remaining years on Skilled Worker Visa
Student Visa (UK).timelineToCitizenship: 9–13 years from starting studies (ILR + 12 months hold + naturalisation processing)
Student Visa (UK).probabilityNote: Approximately 20–25% of international students in the UK eventually obtain ILR. Pipeline attrition points: (1) Graduate Route approval is ~95%+ for eligible UK graduates; (2) Switching to Skilled Worker requires a job offer at £38,700+ — many graduates in arts, humanities, and social sciences struggle to meet this threshold; (3) Maintaining continuous qualifying employment and sponsorship for the full 5-year period; (4) Life in the UK test and English language requirements. Source: Home Office Transparency Data, Migration Advisory Committee student immigration analysis, Oxford Migration Observatory.

Graduate Route Visa (UK).fullName: Graduate Route Visa – Post-Study Open Work Permission
Graduate Route Visa (UK).description: The Graduate Route is the UK's post-study work visa, introduced in 2021. Granted to international students who have completed a UK bachelor's, master's, or PhD at a licensed sponsor institution. Duration: 2 years (bachelor's and master's), 3 years (doctoral). No employer sponsorship required — open work rights for any employer, sector, or salary. Time on the Graduate Route counts toward the 5-year ILR qualifying period when the applicant subsequently switches to a Skilled Worker or other qualifying visa. Cannot be extended beyond its initial duration.
Graduate Route Visa (UK).pathToPR: Graduate from UK institution → Apply for Graduate Route (within 2 years of graduation) → Work in UK with open rights → Switch to Skilled Worker Visa (requires £38,700+ job offer from licensed sponsor) → Complete 5-year ILR qualifying period (counting Graduate Route time) → Apply for ILR
Graduate Route Visa (UK).pathToCitizenship: Graduate Route → Skilled Worker Visa → ILR → 12 months ILR hold → Naturalisation → British Citizen
Graduate Route Visa (UK).timelineToPR: 3–6 years from receiving Graduate Route to ILR (2–3 years on Graduate Route + remaining qualifying time on Skilled Worker Visa)
Graduate Route Visa (UK).probabilityNote: The probability from the Graduate Route stage is higher than from the Student Visa stage because these are committed graduates. Graduate Route approval rate is ~95%+ for eligible graduates. The key bottleneck is switching to Skilled Worker — approximately 60% of Graduate Route holders find an employer offering £38,700+. Of those who switch to Skilled Worker, approximately 75% complete the full ILR qualifying period without interruption. The 39% reflects the combined probability accounting for those who cannot find qualifying employment or choose to leave. Source: Home Office Graduate Route statistics, ONS international student employment data.

Skilled Worker Visa (UK).fullName: Skilled Worker Visa (UK) → Indefinite Leave to Remain
Skilled Worker Visa (UK).description: The Skilled Worker Visa (replacing Tier 2 General in 2021) is the UK's main employer-sponsored skilled worker route. Requires a job offer from a UK employer with a Home Office Sponsor Licence, at or above the job's going rate (SOC code minimum) and a general salary threshold of £38,700/year (April 2024). Granted for up to 5 years and renewable. After 5 continuous years on qualifying visas, applicants can apply for ILR. Dependants (spouse, children) can accompany the main applicant and receive work rights.
Skilled Worker Visa (UK).pathToPR: Secure job offer from UK Sponsor Licence employer → Obtain Certificate of Sponsorship (CoS) → Apply for Skilled Worker Visa → Work in UK for 5 cumulative qualifying years → Pass Life in the UK Test → Meet English language requirement → Apply for ILR → Receive ILR
Skilled Worker Visa (UK).pathToCitizenship: Skilled Worker Visa → ILR → Hold ILR 12 months → Apply for naturalisation (Form AN) → Attend citizenship ceremony → British Citizen
Skilled Worker Visa (UK).timelineToPR: 5 years qualifying period (can include time on Graduate Route or other qualifying visas) + 3–6 months ILR processing
Skilled Worker Visa (UK).timelineToCitizenship: 6–7 years from first Skilled Worker Visa (5 years + 12 months ILR + processing)
Skilled Worker Visa (UK).probabilityNote: Skilled Worker Visa approval rate is approximately 95% for applications meeting requirements. However, the overall probability from receiving a Skilled Worker Visa to ILR is approximately 55–60%, reflecting that approximately 30–35% of holders leave the UK, have sponsorship withdrawn, switch to a non-qualifying visa, or exceed the absence allowance before reaching 5 years. The high salary threshold (£38,700+) filters out lower-paid applicants at entry. Source: Home Office Skilled Worker Visa transparency data, Migration Advisory Committee reports.

Global Talent Visa (UK).fullName: Global Talent Visa – Leaders in Science, Arts, Engineering, Digital, Humanities
Global Talent Visa (UK).description: The Global Talent Visa allows exceptionally talented or promising individuals to live and work in the UK without a job offer. Applicants must be endorsed by a designated competent body (e.g. The Royal Society for STEM, British Academy for humanities, Arts Council for arts, UKRI for research). Two tiers: Exceptional Talent (proven track record) and Exceptional Promise (emerging leader). ILR after 3 years (Talent) or 5 years (Promise). No salary threshold, no employer required.
Global Talent Visa (UK).pathToPR: Apply for endorsement from relevant designated body → If endorsed, apply for Global Talent Visa (3 years, extendable) → Work in UK in expert field → After 3 years (Talent) or 5 years (Promise) of qualifying presence → Apply for ILR → Receive ILR
Global Talent Visa (UK).pathToCitizenship: Global Talent Visa → ILR → 12 months hold → Naturalisation → British Citizen
Global Talent Visa (UK).timelineToPR: 3–5 years from Global Talent Visa to ILR (faster for Exceptional Talent)
Global Talent Visa (UK).timelineToCitizenship: 4–6 years from Global Talent Visa grant
Global Talent Visa (UK).probabilityNote: Global Talent Visa endorsement rates vary significantly by endorsing body and discipline. Endorsement approval rates are typically 15–50% depending on the body. Once endorsed, visa approval is ~98%. The 70% probability reflects the full pipeline from applying for endorsement to receiving ILR. Source: Home Office Global Talent Visa statistics, designated body annual reports.

Family Visa (UK).fullName: UK Family Visa – Spouse, Civil Partner, or Unmarried Partner
Family Visa (UK).description: For spouses, civil partners, and unmarried partners (relationship 2+ years) of British citizens or persons settled in the UK. The sponsor must meet the financial requirement (earning £29,000+/year from April 2024; previously £18,600). Initially granted for 30 months (2.5 years), then extended for another 30 months. After 5 years in the UK on the family route, applicants can apply for ILR. Spouses of British citizens have access to the faster 3-year naturalisation route.
Family Visa (UK).pathToPR: UK partner submits sponsor evidence → Apply for Family Visa (30 months) → Live with UK partner → Extend for another 30 months → After 5 years total → Pass Life in the UK Test → Apply for ILR → Receive ILR
Family Visa (UK).pathToCitizenship: Family Visa → ILR → Hold ILR 12 months (or use 3-year spouse naturalisation route if married to British citizen) → Naturalisation → British Citizen
Family Visa (UK).timelineToCitizenship: 6–7 years from initial Family Visa (spouses of British citizens: as few as 4–5 years via 3-year naturalisation route)
Family Visa (UK).probabilityNote: Family Visa application approval rate is approximately 80–85%. Primary refusal reasons: financial requirement not met (most common — sponsors must earn £29,000+), inability to prove a genuine relationship, or the applicant's poor immigration history. The financial threshold increase from £18,600 to £29,000 in April 2024 is expected to reduce approvals. Of applicants who receive the initial 30-month Family Visa, approximately 92% successfully extend and reach ILR. Source: Home Office Family Visa transparency data, Migration Observatory UK family migration analysis.

UK Ancestry Visa.fullName: UK Ancestry Visa – Commonwealth Nationals with UK-Born Grandparent
UK Ancestry Visa.description: Available exclusively to Commonwealth nationals (Australian, Canadian, Indian, Pakistani, South African, Jamaican, etc.) who have at least one grandparent born in the UK (including Channel Islands and Isle of Man). No job offer and no salary threshold required — applicants need to intend to work and be able to support themselves. Granted for 5 years with open work rights. After 5 years continuous residence, holders can apply for ILR. Partners and children can accompany on a Dependant visa.
UK Ancestry Visa.pathToPR: Prove Commonwealth nationality and UK-born grandparent → Apply for UK Ancestry Visa (5 years, no employer needed) → Work in the UK in any role → Not exceed 180 days absence per year → Pass Life in the UK Test → Apply for ILR after 5 years → Receive ILR
UK Ancestry Visa.pathToCitizenship: UK Ancestry Visa → ILR → 12 months hold → Naturalisation → British Citizen
UK Ancestry Visa.timelineToPR: 5 years on UK Ancestry Visa + 3–6 months ILR processing
UK Ancestry Visa.timelineToCitizenship: ~7 years from arrival (5 years ancestry + 12 months ILR hold + 3–6 months naturalisation)
UK Ancestry Visa.probabilityNote: UK Ancestry Visa approval rate is approximately 87–90% for eligible applicants (Commonwealth national, UK-born grandparent, intend to work). The lower overall 75% probability reflects that a significant portion of ancestry visa holders leave the UK before completing the 5-year ILR qualifying period — typically young Commonwealth nationals who use the visa for work experience rather than permanent settlement. Of those who intend to settle, ILR approval is ~93%. Source: Home Office UK Ancestry Visa statistics, ONS migration data.

ILR (any qualifying visa route).fullName: Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR) – Standard 5-Year Naturalisation Route
ILR (any qualifying visa route).description: Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR) is the UK's permanent residence status, granting the right to live and work in the UK without immigration restrictions. ILR holders can apply for British naturalisation after holding ILR for 12 months. To obtain ILR, applicants must have spent 5 continuous years in the UK on a qualifying visa (Skilled Worker, Global Talent, Family, Ancestry, etc.) with absences not exceeding 180 days in any 12-month period.
ILR (any qualifying visa route).pathToPR: N/A — ILR is the permanent residence stage itself.
ILR (any qualifying visa route).pathToCitizenship: Hold ILR → Wait 12 months → Not exceed 90 days absence in the 12-month period before applying → Pass Life in the UK Test → Meet English language requirement (B1 CEFR) → Submit naturalisation application (Form AN) → Attend citizenship ceremony → Take Oath/Affirmation → British Citizen
ILR (any qualifying visa route).timelineToCitizenship: 12–18 months from receiving ILR to citizenship
ILR (any qualifying visa route).probabilityNote: British naturalisation approval rate is approximately 83–87%. Main refusal reasons: 'good character' failures (criminal convictions, immigration offences in the previous 10 years), failure to meet the physical presence requirement (exceeding 450 days abroad in 5 years), failure of the Life in the UK test, or English language requirement not met. Approximately 130,000–180,000 people are naturalised as British citizens each year. Source: Home Office Transparency Data on Naturalisation, Office for National Statistics immigration statistics.

ILR (Family Visa route).fullName: British Citizenship via Marriage – 3-Year Spouse Route
ILR (Family Visa route).description: Spouses and civil partners of British citizens who have lived in the UK for 3 years can apply for naturalisation after receiving ILR. The absence allowance is 270 days over 3 years (rather than 450 over 5 years), with no more than 90 days absent in the final 12-month period. The marriage must be ongoing at time of application — not just at ILR grant. This is the fastest legal route to British citizenship for foreign nationals married to a British citizen.
ILR (Family Visa route).pathToPR: Family Visa (partner of British citizen) → Two 30-month stages → ILR
ILR (Family Visa route).pathToCitizenship: Family Visa → ILR → Apply for naturalisation (after 3 years UK residence and holding ILR) → Life in the UK test → Citizenship ceremony → British Citizen
ILR (Family Visa route).timelineToPR: See Family Visa pathway for ILR timeline
ILR (Family Visa route).timelineToCitizenship: 3 years UK residence + ILR + 3–6 months naturalisation processing = ~4–5 years total
ILR (Family Visa route).probabilityNote: Spouse-of-British-citizen naturalisation applications have an approval rate of approximately 86–90%. Main denial reasons are good character issues (particularly prior immigration offences) or the marriage having ended by the time of application. Source: Home Office naturalisation data, Migration Observatory.

British Citizenship by Descent.description: A person born outside the UK is automatically a British citizen by descent if at least one parent was a British citizen 'otherwise than by descent' at the time of the person's birth — meaning the parent was born in the UK, naturalised in the UK, or registered as a UK citizen in the UK. The first-generation limit applies: a British citizen by descent cannot automatically pass citizenship to their own children born abroad. A discretionary registration process exists for second-generation children born abroad, but it is not automatic. The UK allows dual citizenship in most cases.
British Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — citizenship by descent bypasses PR entirely.
British Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm parent was a British citizen 'otherwise than by descent' at time of your birth → Gather documents (parent's British passport or naturalisation certificate, your birth certificate) → Apply for a British passport (as proof of citizenship) → British Citizen
British Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists automatically from birth; passport processing 3–8 weeks
British Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: For those who genuinely qualify (parent was British 'otherwise than by descent' at time of birth), passport/registration approval is approximately 93–96%. Main issues: the parent was themselves a British citizen by descent (triggering the first-generation limit), documentation of the parent's citizenship status at the relevant date, or parentage disputes. Source: Home Office British Nationality Act guidance, UK Visas and Immigration data.

permanentResidence.officialName: Permanent Resident Visa (永住者)
criteria: Have lived continuously in Japan for 10 years (including at least 5 years on a work or residence status visa) — reduced to 5 years for Highly Skilled Professional (HSP) 70+ points holders, or 3 years for HSP 80+ points holders
Demonstrate good conduct (no criminal history, no immigration violations, payment of taxes and social insurance)
Have sufficient assets or skills to support yourself independently
It is recognized that granting permanent residence would be in the interest of Japan
Your continued residence in Japan is considered beneficial to Japan (considered holistically by immigration authorities)

routes.name: Student Visa → Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa Pipeline
routes.description: The standard path for international students. After graduating from a Japanese university, students switch to a work visa — most commonly the Engineer/Specialist in Humanities/International Services visa (技術・人文知識・国際業務). Accumulating HSP points during this period (language ability, degree level, salary, age) can qualify holders for the faster HSP PR route in 3–5 years; without HSP status the general 10-year continuous residence rule applies.
routes.estimatedDuration: 8–15 years: 2–4 years study + remaining years on work visa to reach 10-year PR threshold (or 5–7 years if HSP points qualify)

routes.name: Highly Skilled Professional (HSP) Points System → PR
routes.description: Japan's points-based fast-track PR route. Points are awarded for academic background, professional career, annual salary, age, Japanese language ability, and bonus factors (working for a designated innovative company, Japanese university graduate, etc.). 70+ points = PR after 5 years; 80+ points = PR after 3 years. The HSP visa (高度専門職) is valid for 5 years and grants expanded privileges including multiple activities, longer spouse work rights, and faster PR.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3–5 years from receiving HSP visa to PR (3 years if 80+ points, 5 years if 70–79 points)

routes.name: Engineer/Specialist Visa → Permanent Residence (10-Year Route)
routes.description: The standard long-term employment route. Holders of work visas (Engineer/Specialist in Humanities/International Services, Skilled Labor, Intra-company Transferee, etc.) who continuously reside in Japan for 10 years — with at least 5 of those years on a work-type visa — can apply for permanent residence. Requires demonstrating tax compliance, stable income, and good conduct throughout the period.
routes.estimatedDuration: 10 years of continuous residence (at least 5 years on a work visa) + several months PR processing

routes.name: Family – Spouse of Japanese National Visa
routes.description: Spouses of Japanese nationals receive a special residence status (配偶者等) and qualify for PR under a shortened residence requirement: married 3+ years AND at least 1 year of continuous residence in Japan. Alternatively, 3 years of continuous residence in Japan while married suffices. This is the fastest PR route for non-Japanese nationals and reflects Japan's special consideration for family unity.
routes.estimatedDuration: 1–3 years of qualifying continuous residence after marriage to a Japanese national

routes.name: Investor/Business Manager Visa → PR
routes.description: For foreign nationals managing a business in Japan (経営・管理). Requires establishing or managing a business with at least 5 million yen invested or 2 full-time employees. After 10 years of continuous residence on qualifying status (including time on Business Manager Visa), PR becomes available. There is no separate investor PR fast-track in Japan — the general 10-year rule applies.
routes.estimatedDuration: 10 years of continuous residence on qualifying visa status, including time on Business Manager Visa

criteria: Have continuously resided in Japan for at least 5 years (or shorter for spouses of Japanese nationals — 3 years if married 3+ years, or 1 year if born in Japan or if Japanese parent)
Be 18 years of age or older
Be of good conduct (no criminal record or history of threatening public safety)
Be financially stable (able to support yourself and dependants)
Have never plotted against or been involved in overthrowing Japan's constitution or government

IMPORTANT: Japan does not recognize dual citizenship — you must renounce all other nationalities upon naturalization. This is a significant and often decisive barrier for applicants from countries that do not allow renunciation or where losing citizenship has major consequences.

routes.name: Naturalization – General (5-Year Continuous Residence)
routes.description: The standard path to Japanese citizenship. After 5 years of continuous residence in Japan (Permanent Resident status is NOT required — the 5 years can be on work or long-term resident visas), apply for naturalization (帰化申請) at the regional Legal Affairs Bureau. The process includes interviews, background checks, document submission over several months, and requires renunciation of all other nationalities.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years continuous residence in Japan + 12–24 months for naturalization application processing

routes.name: Naturalization via Marriage to Japanese Citizen (3-Year Rule)
routes.description: Spouses of Japanese nationals can naturalize after just 3 years of marriage AND 1 year of continuous residence in Japan (or 1 year of marriage AND 3 years of residence). This shortened path reflects Japan's recognition of family-based integration. All other naturalization requirements — good conduct, financial stability, renunciation of other nationalities — still apply.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3 years of marriage + 1 year continuous residence (or 1 year marriage + 3 years residence) + 12–24 months naturalization processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born to Japanese Parent)
routes.description: Japan applies jus sanguinis — children born to at least one Japanese parent acquire Japanese citizenship at birth, regardless of where they are born. If born abroad, the birth must be registered at a Japanese consulate within 3 months to preserve citizenship. Note: children born abroad who hold dual citizenship are required to choose one nationality by age 22 (or within 2 years of acquiring a foreign nationality if after age 20). Japan does not recognize dual citizenship in adults.
routes.estimatedDuration: Automatic from birth; consulate birth registration within 3 months. Citizenship choice required by age 22.

Student Visa (Japan).fullName: Japanese Student Visa (留学) → Permanent Residence Pipeline
Student Visa (Japan).description: The Japanese Student Visa (留学ビザ) allows international students to study at Japanese universities, colleges, vocational schools, and language schools. It does not directly lead to PR but is the entry point for a long-term pipeline: graduate (preferably from a Japanese university) → switch to Engineer/Specialist in Humanities visa → accumulate HSP points during work years → qualify for HSP-accelerated PR in 3–5 years, or complete the standard 10-year general route. Japanese language proficiency (JLPT N2 or above) significantly boosts HSP points and employability. Japan actively encourages international students from Japanese universities to remain and work.
Student Visa (Japan).pathToPR: Student Visa → Graduate from Japanese institution → Switch to Engineer/Specialist in Humanities/International Services Visa → Work in Japan → Accumulate HSP points (degree, salary, language, age) → If 70+ HSP points: apply for HSP Visa → PR after 5 years (or 3 years with 80+ points). If not pursuing HSP: maintain 10 continuous years residence → Apply for Permanent Resident Visa
Student Visa (Japan).pathToCitizenship: Student Visa → Graduate → Work Visa → Accumulate 5 years total continuous Japan residence (student time counts fully) → Apply for naturalization directly — PR is NOT required for citizenship → Pass Japanese language interview → Good conduct declaration → Ministry of Justice review (12–24 months) → Renounce prior nationality → Japanese Citizen
Student Visa (Japan).timelineToPR: 8–15 years from starting studies: 2–4 years study + 3–5 years HSP fast-track, or 6–8 more years on regular work visa to reach 10-year threshold
Student Visa (Japan).timelineToCitizenship: 6–9 years from starting studies (5 years total Japan residence including student time → direct naturalization, NO PR required first) + 12–24 months processing
Student Visa (Japan).probabilityNote: The 16% citizenship probability reflects the shortened direct naturalization path (5 years total Japan residence, student time counts — no PR needed first), but is tempered by Japan's strict single-citizenship requirement (renunciation of prior nationality is mandatory). For nationals of countries where losing one's original passport is costly (US, EU, etc.), renunciation is a decisive barrier. Japan's application approval rate for technically qualifying applicants is approximately 85%, but the renunciation barrier means most long-term residents on work visas never apply. The 20% PR probability reflects the difficulty of accumulating 10 years continuous qualifying residence on work visas given Japan's strict renewal requirements and the high departure rate among foreign workers. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan naturalization statistics.

Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.fullName: Engineer/Specialist in Humanities/International Services Visa (技術・人文知識・国際業務)
Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.description: The most common work visa in Japan for white-collar foreign workers. Covers three activity types: (1) Engineer — IT, engineering, R&D roles requiring university-level technical knowledge; (2) Specialist in Humanities — accounting, law, business management, marketing requiring humanities or social science expertise; (3) International Services — roles requiring foreign cultural background such as language teachers, translators, liaison roles. Requires a job offer from a Japanese employer and a degree (bachelor's or above) or 10+ years of relevant work experience. Granted for 3 months to 5 years; renewable indefinitely. The key path to PR is either accumulating 10 continuous years or building HSP points to switch to the HSP visa.
Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.pathToPR: Secure job offer from Japanese employer → Apply for visa → Work in Japan → Renew visa repeatedly → After 10 continuous years (at least 5 on work visa) → Apply for Permanent Resident Visa (永住許可申請) → Receive PR. OR: Accumulate HSP points (70+) → Switch to HSP visa → PR in 3–5 years
Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.pathToCitizenship: Engineer/Specialist Visa → 10 years continuous residence (or 5 years on HSP) → PR → 5 more years? Actually can naturalize after 5 years total continuous residence even without PR → Apply for naturalization → Renounce other nationalities → Japanese Citizen
Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.timelineToPR: 10 years of continuous residence on qualifying visas (at least 5 years on work status) + several months PR processing. HSP fast-track: 3–5 years from switching to HSP
Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.timelineToCitizenship: 5 years continuous residence in Japan (total, not necessarily all on one visa type) + 12–24 months naturalization processing
Engineer/Specialist in Humanities Visa (.probabilityNote: The 35% probability reflects the subset of Engineer/Specialist visa holders who ultimately obtain PR. The 10-year requirement means significant attrition — many holders return home, change jobs that require visa changes, or leave Japan voluntarily before reaching the threshold. Of those who remain 10+ years, PR approval rate is approximately 85–90%. The 12% citizenship probability is extremely low primarily due to Japan's strict no-dual-citizenship policy. Many holders who would otherwise qualify for naturalization decline because renouncing their home country citizenship is unacceptable to them personally or legally. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan immigration data, Immigration Services Agency statistics.

Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).fullName: Highly Skilled Professional Visa (高度専門職) – Points-Based Fast-Track PR
Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).description: Japan's points-based visa for highly qualified foreign professionals, designed to attract global talent. Points are calculated using the Ministry of Justice scoring system across three categories: (1) 高度学術研究活動 (Advanced Academic Research) — researchers and professors; (2) 高度専門・技術活動 (Advanced Specialized/Technical) — engineers, IT specialists, business managers; (3) 高度経営・管理活動 (Advanced Business Management) — executives and managers. Points are awarded for: academic degree (up to 30 pts), work experience (up to 15 pts), annual salary (up to 40 pts for 30M+ yen), age (up to 15 pts), Japanese language ability (up to 15 pts), bonus factors (innovative company, Japanese university graduate, government-designated research project). Minimum 70 points for HSP visa. HSP holders can obtain PR after 5 years (70–79 points) or 3 years (80+ points). HSP also grants broader activity permissions and longer spouse work rights.
Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).pathToPR: Calculate HSP points score (use Ministry of Justice online calculator) → If 70+: apply for HSP visa (高度専門職1号) → Work in Japan on HSP → After 5 years (70–79 pts) or 3 years (80+ pts) continuous HSP residence → Apply for Permanent Resident Visa → Receive PR. Can also upgrade to HSP 2号 (renewable visa without activity restrictions) before applying for PR
Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).pathToCitizenship: HSP Visa → Accumulate 5 years total continuous Japan residence (including any prior work/student time in Japan) → Direct naturalization application (NO PR required) → Japanese language interview → Ministry of Justice review (12–24 months) → Renounce prior nationality → Japanese Citizen. Alternatively: HSP → PR (3–5 years via HSP fast track) → citizenship any time after PR.
Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).timelineToPR: 3–5 years from receiving HSP visa to PR (3 years if 80+ points, 5 years if 70–79 points). Add time to accumulate HSP eligibility first (typically 2–5 years working in Japan on a regular work visa).
Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).timelineToCitizenship: 6–9 years total from first arriving in Japan (5 years any lawful residence → direct naturalization) OR 4–8 years if 5-year threshold reached on HSP/prior work period
Highly Skilled Professional Visa (Japan).probabilityNote: HSP visa holders have a significantly higher PR success rate than general work visa holders because: (1) they typically earn above-median salaries and hold advanced qualifications; (2) the HSP PR timeline (3–5 years) is far more achievable than the 10-year general route. HSP application approval rate is approximately 90–93%. Of HSP holders, approximately 65–70% eventually obtain PR. The 18% citizenship probability remains low due to Japan's dual citizenship prohibition — even highly integrated professionals often refuse to renounce their home country citizenship. Source: Ministry of Justice HSP statistics, Immigration Services Agency data.

Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).fullName: Skilled Labor Visa (技能) – Traditional Crafts and Specialized Trades
Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).description: The Skilled Labor Visa covers specific occupations requiring practical skills traditionally valued in Japan that are not covered by the Engineer/Specialist visa: Chinese cuisine chefs, Western cuisine chefs, Japanese cuisine chefs, aircraft pilots, sports instructors, animal trainers, building maintenance workers, and jewelry/precious metals craftspeople. Requires a job offer from a Japanese employer and significant work experience in the field (typically 10+ years for chefs). The path to PR follows the same 10-year continuous residence rule as other work visas. Very few visas are issued annually in this category compared to Engineer/Specialist.
Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).pathToPR: Secure job offer in qualifying skilled trade → Apply for Skilled Labor Visa → Work in Japan → Renew visa → After 10 continuous years of qualifying residence (at least 5 on work status) → Apply for Permanent Resident Visa → Receive PR
Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).pathToCitizenship: Skilled Labor Visa → 10 years PR-eligible residence → PR → Naturalization (5 years continuous Japan residence, renounce other nationalities) → Japanese Citizen
Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).timelineToPR: 10 years of continuous qualifying residence + several months PR application processing
Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).timelineToCitizenship: 5 years continuous Japan residence (can include time before PR) + 12–24 months naturalization processing
Skilled Labor Visa (Japan).probabilityNote: The 30% probability reflects a relatively low conversion rate from Skilled Labor Visa to PR — many skilled labor workers (especially chefs) come to Japan for limited periods and return to their home country. The 10-year requirement is particularly burdensome for this group. Of those who do pursue long-term residence, PR approval is ~85%. The 10% citizenship probability is reduced further by Japan's dual citizenship prohibition and the generally lower integration rates in this visa category. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan visa statistics.

Spouse of Japanese National Visa.fullName: Spouse/Child of Japanese National Visa (日本人の配偶者等) → Permanent Residence
Spouse of Japanese National Visa.description: A special residence status granted to spouses, children (biological and legally adopted), and special permanent residents' spouses of Japanese nationals. This status grants open work rights (any job, any employer, no restrictions). For PR purposes, the spouse route has a shortened qualifying period: married 3+ years AND 1 year continuous residence in Japan, OR married any duration AND 3 years continuous residence in Japan. PR application success rate is high for genuine relationships. This is also the fastest pathway to citizenship (naturalization after 3 years marriage + 1 year residence).
Spouse of Japanese National Visa.pathToPR: Marry Japanese national → Apply for Spouse of Japanese National Visa → Live in Japan continuously → Meet shortened PR threshold (married 3+ years AND 1 year Japan residence) → Apply for Permanent Resident Visa (永住許可) → Receive PR
Spouse of Japanese National Visa.pathToCitizenship: Spouse Visa → Naturalize after 3 years marriage + 1 year Japan residence (or 1 year marriage + 3 years Japan residence) → Submit naturalization application at Legal Affairs Bureau → Interview and document review → Renounce other nationalities upon approval → Japanese Citizen
Spouse of Japanese National Visa.timelineToPR: 1–3 years: married 3+ years AND 1 year Japan residence qualifies. If married less than 3 years: need 3 years continuous Japan residence
Spouse of Japanese National Visa.timelineToCitizenship: 3–5 years from marriage + 12–24 months naturalization processing = approximately 4–7 years from marriage
Spouse of Japanese National Visa.probabilityNote: Spouse of Japanese National visa holders have a high PR approval rate (~85–90%) for those who meet the shortened residence requirements, as Japan's immigration authorities strongly prioritize family unity. The 72% overall probability (vs. the ~85–90% application approval rate) reflects the fraction of spouse visa holders who actually apply for PR — many couples divorce or relocate before the qualifying period, and some prefer to remain on the spouse visa (which already grants open work rights). The 28% citizenship probability is low because many foreign spouses of Japanese nationals are unwilling to renounce their home country citizenship. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan immigration statistics.

Business Manager Visa (Japan).fullName: Business Manager Visa (経営・管理) → Permanent Residence
Business Manager Visa (Japan).description: For foreign nationals who intend to manage a business in Japan. Requirements: the business must be established in Japan (or the applicant takes over an existing business), with either (a) invested capital or assets of at least 5 million yen, or (b) at least 2 full-time employees (not including the visa holder). Common applicants include entrepreneurs, franchise operators, and corporate executives transferred to Japan. Visa is granted for 4 months to 5 years, renewable. The PR path follows the standard 10-year continuous residence rule — there is no investor fast-track in Japan comparable to Australia's Business Innovation visa.
Business Manager Visa (Japan).pathToPR: Establish or acquire a qualifying business in Japan (5M yen capital or 2+ employees) → Apply for Business Manager Visa → Operate business in Japan → Renew visa → After 10 continuous years of qualifying residence (at least 5 on Business Manager status) → Apply for Permanent Resident Visa → Receive PR
Business Manager Visa (Japan).pathToCitizenship: Business Manager Visa → 10 years PR-eligible residence → PR → Naturalization (5 years continuous Japan residence, renounce other nationalities) → Japanese Citizen
Business Manager Visa (Japan).timelineToPR: 10 years of continuous qualifying residence (at least 5 on Business Manager status) + PR processing time
Business Manager Visa (Japan).timelineToCitizenship: 5 years continuous Japan residence + 12–24 months naturalization processing
Business Manager Visa (Japan).probabilityNote: The 38% probability reflects that business failures, visa non-renewal (if the business ceases to meet criteria), or voluntary departure before the 10-year threshold eliminate a large portion of Business Manager visa holders from the PR pipeline. Japan's immigration authorities scrutinize business genuineness carefully — businesses that fail to maintain the required capital or employees can result in visa non-renewal. Of those who successfully maintain qualifying status for 10 years, PR approval is ~85–90%. The 12% citizenship probability is low primarily due to Japan's dual citizenship prohibition. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan immigration statistics.

Permanent Resident Visa (Japan).description: Holding Japanese Permanent Residence (永住者) does not automatically lead to citizenship — PR in Japan means indefinite right to reside without visa renewal, open work rights, and simplified re-entry procedures. To naturalize, a PR holder must apply separately and meet the standard naturalization requirements, including 5 years continuous residence in Japan (PRs easily meet this), good conduct, financial stability, and — critically — renunciation of all other nationalities. Japan does not allow dual citizenship for naturalized citizens. This no-dual-citizenship rule is the primary reason many eligible PR holders choose not to naturalize.
Permanent Resident Visa (Japan).pathToPR: N/A — you must already hold Permanent Resident status to follow this citizenship pathway.
Permanent Resident Visa (Japan).pathToCitizenship: Hold Japanese PR (永住者) → Apply for naturalization (帰化申請) at the regional Legal Affairs Bureau → Submit documents (ID, residence history, employment/income proof, criminal record, foreign nationality documents) → Interview with immigration examiner → Background investigation (6–12 months) → Receive approval notice → Renounce all other nationalities → Submit renunciation certificate → Officially become a Japanese national → Receive Japanese family register (戸籍)
Permanent Resident Visa (Japan).timelineToCitizenship: 12–24 months from filing naturalization application to becoming a Japanese citizen
Permanent Resident Visa (Japan).probabilityNote: The 72% probability reflects the naturalization approval rate among PR holders who actually apply — approximately 95% of those who submit a complete naturalization application are approved (Ministry of Justice data). However, the 72% significantly discounts the large fraction of PR holders who choose NOT to apply because they cannot or will not renounce their other citizenship. Japan naturalizes approximately 8,000–12,000 people per year out of ~800,000+ permanent residents, suggesting only ~1–1.5% of PR holders naturalize annually. The probability is also reduced by: application withdrawals (applicants who discover mid-process they cannot renounce), rare denials for character or financial reasons, and the complex documentation requirements. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan naturalization statistics.

Japanese Citizenship by Descent.fullName: Japanese Citizenship by Descent (Jus Sanguinis – 血統主義)
Japanese Citizenship by Descent.description: Japan applies strict jus sanguinis — citizenship is transmitted through blood, not place of birth. A child born to at least one Japanese parent acquires Japanese citizenship at birth, regardless of where the birth occurs.
