If born abroad, the birth must be registered at a Japanese consulate within 3 months of birth, or the Japanese citizenship may be lost (an exception exists if registered before age 22). IMPORTANT: Japan does not officially recognize dual citizenship for adults. Children who hold dual citizenship (e.g., born abroad to a Japanese parent in a jus soli country) must choose one nationality by age 22 — or within 2 years of reaching adulthood if they acquired the second nationality later. Children born in Japan to non-Japanese parents do NOT automatically acquire Japanese citizenship (Japan does not practice birthright citizenship/jus soli).
Japanese Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — Japanese citizenship by descent bypasses PR entirely.
Japanese Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm at least one parent was a Japanese national at time of birth → If born abroad: register birth at Japanese consulate within 3 months → Receive Japanese family register entry (戸籍) → Confirmed Japanese citizen. → By age 22: formally choose Japanese nationality and renounce other nationalities (or lose Japanese citizenship by failing to choose)
Japanese Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth; consulate registration required within 3 months if born abroad. Nationality choice required by age 22.
Japanese Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: For those who genuinely meet the eligibility criteria (born to a Japanese parent), confirmation of Japanese citizenship is approximately 88–92% successful. The main reasons for non-confirmation: failure to register the birth at a consulate within the required timeframe (resulting in loss of Japanese citizenship), the parent having already lost Japanese citizenship before the child's birth (e.g., mother who naturalized abroad), or failure to make the nationality choice by age 22 (resulting in loss of Japanese citizenship). The 10% reduction also accounts for individuals who voluntarily choose not to exercise their Japanese citizenship. Source: Ministry of Justice Japan Nationality Law (国籍法), consular registration data.

permanentResidence.officialName: Singapore Permanent Resident (PR)
criteria: Hold an eligible work pass (Employment Pass, S Pass, EntrePass) or qualifying relationship (spouse/child of SC or PR) or approved investor scheme
PR applications are entirely discretionary — Singapore's ICA (Immigration and Checkpoints Authority) does not publish a points score or fixed approval criteria

Key factors considered: length of employment in Singapore, educational qualifications, salary level, economic contribution to Singapore, immediate family ties to Singapore citizens or PRs, and integration into Singapore society
No criminal record and good character
Must have contributed to Singapore's CPF (Central Provident Fund) if on an S Pass or eligible work permit

routes.name: Student's Pass → Employment Pass Pipeline
routes.description: The standard path for international students at Singapore universities (NUS, NTU, SMU, SUTD, etc.). Unlike Australia or the UK, Singapore has no dedicated post-study work visa — graduates must secure a job offer paying at least S$5,600/month to qualify for an Employment Pass (EP). After typically 2–4 years on the EP, professionals can apply for PR, though approval is entirely at ICA's discretion. Graduates from Singapore's top universities are looked upon favourably.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–10 years: 3–4 years study + 2–4 years on Employment Pass before PR application, though PR is not guaranteed

routes.name: Employment Pass → Permanent Residence
routes.description: The primary route for foreign professionals. The Employment Pass (EP) is for professionals, managers, and executives earning at least S$5,600/month (S$6,000 for financial sector as of 2023). After 2–3 years on EP, applicants may apply for PR. There is no fixed qualifying period or points score — ICA evaluates each application holistically. The COMPASS (Complementarity Assessment Framework) points system applies to new EP applications but not directly to PR applications.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2–4 years on Employment Pass before PR application; PR processing takes 6–12 months

routes.name: S Pass → Permanent Residence
routes.description: For mid-level skilled workers earning at least S$3,150/month (S$3,650 for financial sector). The S Pass is subject to a foreign worker quota (up to 15% of a company's headcount in most sectors). PR is possible but harder to obtain from S Pass than EP, as higher-earning EP holders are generally preferred. PR from S Pass typically requires more years of employment and very strong employer endorsement.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4–7 years on S Pass before a competitive PR application; PR is significantly harder to obtain than from EP

routes.name: Family – Spouse/Child of Singapore Citizen or PR
routes.description: Spouses and unmarried children (under 21) of Singapore Citizens (SC) can apply directly for PR. Spouses of Singapore Permanent Residents receive a Dependant's Pass and may apply for PR after some time in Singapore. Spouses of SCs typically receive a Long-Term Visit Pass (LTVP or LTVP+) and may apply for PR at any time — approval depends on the relationship's genuineness and the SC spouse's profile. There is no mandatory minimum residency period, though longer ties strengthen the application.
routes.estimatedDuration: 1–5 years: PR available to apply for as soon as you arrive as a spouse of an SC; approval timelines vary widely by ICA

routes.name: Global Investor Programme (GIP) → PR
routes.description: Singapore's investment-based PR scheme for high-net-worth individuals. Requires either: Option A — invest at least S$10 million in a new or existing Singapore business; Option B — invest S$25 million in a GIP-approved fund; Option C — invest S$50 million in a new Singapore-based family office with AUM of at least S$200 million. The GIP is administered by the Singapore Economic Development Board (EDB). PR is granted upon investment approval. GIP investors may also be eligible to apply for Singapore citizenship.
routes.estimatedDuration: 6–12 months from application to PR approval (subject to investment verification)

criteria: Must first hold Singapore Permanent Residence
Generally, must have been a PR for at least 2 years before applying (spouses of Singapore Citizens may apply sooner)
Must intend to reside permanently in Singapore
Good character (no criminal history, no serious immigration violations)
Able to speak and understand basic English or another official language (Malay, Mandarin, Tamil)

IMPORTANT: Singapore does not allow dual citizenship — you must renounce all other nationalities upon becoming a Singapore citizen. All Singapore-born children who hold foreign citizenship must also choose Singapore citizenship by age 21 or lose their Singapore citizenship.
Citizenship is entirely discretionary — Singapore does not guarantee citizenship to any category of PR holder, and there is no fixed qualifying period or points score

routes.name: Naturalization – General (PR + 2-Year Rule)
routes.description: The standard path to Singapore citizenship. After holding PR for at least 2 years, PRs may apply for citizenship. Approval is entirely discretionary — ICA evaluates economic contribution, family ties, integration, and long-term commitment to Singapore. Applicants must renounce all other nationalities upon approval. Singapore naturalizes approximately 18,000–23,000 new citizens per year.
routes.estimatedDuration: 2+ years as PR + 3–6 months citizenship application processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Registration (Spouse of Singapore Citizen)
routes.description: Spouses of Singapore Citizens may apply for citizenship by registration (rather than naturalization) after becoming a PR and demonstrating a genuine, stable marriage. The citizenship-by-registration route for spouses does not require the standard 2-year PR wait — ICA may approve sooner for established couples. Renunciation of all other nationalities is required upon approval.
routes.estimatedDuration: PR granted first (variable timeline) + citizenship application at any time thereafter; total 2–7 years from marriage

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born to Singapore Citizen Parent)
routes.description: A child born anywhere in the world to at least one Singapore citizen parent acquires Singapore citizenship at birth. If born outside Singapore, the birth must be registered at a Singapore consulate or the ICA. All Singapore citizens who hold another citizenship by birth must renounce the foreign citizenship by age 21 — or lose their Singapore citizenship. Singapore does not recognize dual citizenship in adults.
routes.estimatedDuration: Automatic from birth; overseas birth registration required. Nationality choice required by age 21.

Student's Pass (Singapore).fullName: Singapore Student's Pass → Permanent Residence Pipeline
Student's Pass (Singapore).description: The Student's Pass (STP) allows international students to study at Singapore's private schools, polytechnics, Institute of Technical Education (ITE), and autonomous universities (NUS, NTU, SMU, SUTD, SIT, SUSS). Singapore does not have a post-study work visa — after graduating, students must secure a qualifying job offer (S$5,600+/month for an Employment Pass) to remain in Singapore legally. Graduates from Singapore's top universities (especially NUS and NTU, consistently ranked in the world's top 15) are favourably viewed by employers and ICA. PR is not automatic and is determined by ICA on a discretionary, holistic basis after 2–4 years of Employment Pass experience.
Student's Pass (Singapore).pathToPR: Student's Pass → Graduate from Singapore institution → Secure job offer paying S$5,600+/month → Apply for Employment Pass (EP) → Work in Singapore on EP → After 2–4 years, apply for PR (entirely discretionary, ICA holistic assessment) → Receive PR
Student's Pass (Singapore).pathToCitizenship: Student's Pass → EP → PR → Hold PR 2+ years → Apply for citizenship → Renounce other nationalities → Singapore Citizen
Student's Pass (Singapore).timelineToPR: 6–10 years from starting studies: 3–4 years degree + 2–4 years on Employment Pass before competitive PR application
Student's Pass (Singapore).timelineToCitizenship: 9–15 years from starting studies (add 2+ years as PR + citizenship processing)
Student's Pass (Singapore).probabilityNote: The 18% probability reflects the full attrition across the student-to-PR pipeline: (1) only ~60–70% of international students who graduate from Singapore institutions find qualifying EP-level employment (S$5,600+/month); (2) of EP holders, ICA approves approximately 30–45% of PR applications — the rate has declined significantly since 2010 as Singapore tightened PR criteria; (3) PR applications from graduates with only 2–3 years Singapore work experience are often rejected before 4–5 years is reached. Singapore does not publish official PR approval rates, making precise estimates difficult. Sources: ICA Annual Statistics, Ministry of Manpower employment data, NUS/NTU graduate employment surveys, public accounts of PR application outcomes.

Employment Pass (Singapore).description: The Employment Pass is Singapore's main work visa for professionals, managers, and executives. Minimum salary: S$5,600/month (S$6,000 for financial sector, as of September 2023). New EP applications are evaluated under the COMPASS framework (Complementarity Assessment Framework), which scores applicants on salary, qualifications, diversity, and support for local employment. The EP is granted for 1–2 years and is renewable. Unlike many countries, Singapore's path to PR is NOT tied to a points system or fixed years of residency — PR is entirely at ICA's discretion. Key factors ICA weighs: salary level, employer type and reputation, professional qualifications, family ties to SC/PR, length of employment in Singapore, and integration.
Employment Pass (Singapore).pathToPR: Receive Employment Pass → Work in Singapore for 2–4+ years → Build a strong PR application (salary growth, employer endorsement, community ties) → Submit PR application to ICA (Form 4A online) → ICA holistic assessment (6–12 months) → If approved, receive PR In-Principle Approval → Complete formalities → Permanent Resident
Employment Pass (Singapore).pathToCitizenship: EP → PR → Hold PR 2+ years → Apply for citizenship by naturalization → Renounce other nationalities upon approval → Singapore Citizen
Employment Pass (Singapore).timelineToPR: 2–6 years on EP before PR approval (2 years minimum realistic; 4+ years is more typical for a competitive application)
Employment Pass (Singapore).timelineToCitizenship: 5–10 years from first arriving on EP (2–4 years EP + 2+ years PR + citizenship processing)
Employment Pass (Singapore).probabilityNote: The 35% probability reflects ICA's discretionary and selective PR approval process. Singapore's Ministry of Manpower and ICA do not publish PR approval rates, but multiple surveys and professional estimates suggest EP holders have approximately a 30–45% lifetime chance of obtaining PR — much lower than comparable developed countries. Key factors that improve odds: salary above S$10,000+/month, employment at a MNC or strategic industry, spouse/child who is an SC or PR, strong community integration, and applying after 4–5 years rather than 2–3. The 18% citizenship probability reflects Singapore's even more selective citizenship process — Singapore naturalizes approximately 18,000–23,000 people per year out of ~500,000 PRs, and citizenship requires additional years as PR. The no-dual-citizenship rule deters many eligible PRs from applying. Source: ICA Singapore PR/Citizenship statistics (aggregated), Ministry of Manpower work pass data.

S Pass (Singapore).description: The S Pass is for mid-level skilled foreign workers earning at least S$3,150/month (S$3,650 for financial sector). It is subject to a sector-specific quota (15% of total workforce for most sectors, 10% for construction/marine). Unlike the EP, the S Pass does not use the COMPASS framework but has fixed criteria. PR is possible from S Pass but significantly harder to obtain than from EP — ICA generally prioritises higher-earning, higher-skilled EP holders for PR. S Pass holders aiming for PR typically need 5+ years of Singapore employment, a strong income trajectory, and ideally a Singapore citizen or PR family member.
S Pass (Singapore).pathToPR: Receive S Pass → Work in Singapore for 5+ years → Demonstrate consistent income growth → Submit PR application to ICA → If approved: PR. Many S Pass holders first upgrade to an EP (if salary increases sufficiently) before applying for PR
S Pass (Singapore).pathToCitizenship: S Pass → (upgrade to EP if possible) → PR → Hold PR 2+ years → Citizenship application → Renounce other nationalities → Singapore Citizen
S Pass (Singapore).timelineToPR: 5–10 years on S Pass or combined S Pass/EP experience before a realistic PR application
S Pass (Singapore).timelineToCitizenship: 8–15 years total from first arriving on S Pass
S Pass (Singapore).probabilityNote: The 20% probability reflects the lower PR conversion rate from S Pass compared to EP. ICA treats mid-skilled workers more cautiously in PR approvals — Singapore's PR strategy prioritises high-value economic contributors. Many S Pass holders are in sectors (construction, marine, manufacturing) where PR approval is rare. Of those who persist for 5+ years, upgrade to EP, and build strong Singapore ties, PR approval rates improve to approximately 35–40%. The 10% citizenship probability is further reduced because: many S Pass backgrounds don't meet the high bar for citizenship, the no-dual-citizenship rule is a significant deterrent, and many workers prefer to cycle back and forth rather than commit to permanent settlement. Source: ICA statistics, Ministry of Manpower S Pass data.

Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.fullName: Long-Term Visit Pass (LTVP) / Dependant's Pass → PR and Citizenship
Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.description: Spouses of Singapore Citizens (SC) receive a Long-Term Visit Pass (LTVP or LTVP+), which grants the right to live in Singapore and, for LTVP+, to work with a Letter of Consent. Spouses of Singapore Permanent Residents receive a Dependant's Pass (DP). Both categories can apply for PR at any time — for SC spouses, PR is strongly favoured and approval is significantly more likely than for work-pass holders. There is no mandatory minimum residency for LTVP holders to apply for PR. Spouses with LTVP+ status (longer-term, higher commitment) have the highest PR approval rates. For citizenship, SC spouses can apply by registration after receiving PR.
Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.pathToPR: Marry Singapore Citizen or PR → Receive LTVP (SC spouse) or Dependant's Pass (PR spouse) → Settle in Singapore → Apply for PR (can apply immediately for SC spouses, or after some time for PR spouses) → ICA assessment of genuine relationship + SC/PR sponsor's profile → Receive PR
Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.pathToCitizenship: LTVP/DP → PR → Apply for citizenship by registration (SC spouses) or naturalization (PR spouses who become SC later) → Renounce other nationalities → Singapore Citizen
Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.timelineToPR: 6 months – 3 years for SC spouses (PR can be applied for immediately; approval typically within 6–18 months for genuine relationships); 2–5 years for PR spouses
Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.timelineToCitizenship: 3–8 years from marriage for SC spouses (PR + 2 years + processing); longer for PR spouses
Long-Term Visit Pass / Dependant's Pass.probabilityNote: Spouses of Singapore Citizens have significantly higher PR approval rates (~60–70%) than work-pass holders, as family unity is strongly weighted in Singapore's immigration policy. Spouses of Singapore PRs have lower rates (~35–45%) since their sponsor is not a citizen. The 62% reflects the blended probability across both groups. The 35% citizenship probability is higher than work-pass routes because SC spouses are eligible for citizenship by registration (not just naturalization) and ICA views family-unit integration very favourably. The no-dual-citizenship rule still deters some eligible applicants. Source: ICA Singapore family immigration statistics, public PR/citizenship outcome surveys.

Global Investor Programme (GIP).fullName: Global Investor Programme (GIP) – Investment-Based Permanent Residence
Global Investor Programme (GIP).description: Singapore's investment-based PR pathway for high-net-worth individuals and business owners. Administered by the Singapore Economic Development Board (EDB). Three investment options (as of 2023): Option A — invest at least S$10 million in a new or existing Singapore-based business; Option B — invest at least S$25 million in a GIP-approved fund that invests in Singapore companies; Option C — invest at least S$50 million in a new Singapore-based single-family office with AUM of at least S$200 million. Applicants must have a strong business and investment track record. PR is granted to the principal applicant and their immediate family upon approval. GIP PRs can apply for citizenship after 2 years as PRs.
Global Investor Programme (GIP).pathToPR: Submit GIP application to EDB → EDB assesses business track record and investment plan → Interview with EDB → If approved: make the qualifying investment → Receive PR In-Principle Approval → Complete investment → Receive PR for principal applicant and immediate family
Global Investor Programme (GIP).pathToCitizenship: GIP PR → Hold PR for 2+ years → Apply for citizenship by naturalization → Renounce other nationalities → Singapore Citizen
Global Investor Programme (GIP).timelineToPR: 6–12 months from GIP application to PR approval (subject to investment verification)
Global Investor Programme (GIP).timelineToCitizenship: 3–5 years from initial GIP application (PR + 2 years + processing)
Global Investor Programme (GIP).probabilityNote: The 58% probability reflects EDB's selective GIP approval process — applicants must demonstrate a genuine, substantial business track record, not merely financial capacity. EDB rejects GIP applications where the investment track record is weak, the business plan is not credible, or the applicant has compliance issues. Of those who receive GIP PR, nearly all maintain PR (the investment conditions are monitored). The 30% citizenship probability is lower because: many GIP investors are high-net-worth individuals who maintain multiple residencies and are unwilling to renounce other nationalities; Singapore's citizenship is highly valued but the renunciation requirement is a significant barrier for investors with global ties. Source: EDB Singapore GIP programme statistics, public accounts of GIP approvals.

Singapore Permanent Resident (Any Pathwa.fullName: Singapore Citizenship by Naturalization – General Route
Singapore Permanent Resident (Any Pathwa.description: Any Singapore Permanent Resident, regardless of the pathway through which they obtained PR, may apply for citizenship by naturalization after at least 2 years as a PR. Citizenship is entirely at ICA's discretion — there is no automatic entitlement even after many years as a PR. Singapore emphasizes full commitment to Singapore: applicants are expected to be genuinely settled (children attending Singapore schools, paying taxes, participating in community life), and must renounce all other nationalities upon approval. Singapore naturalizes approximately 18,000–23,000 new citizens annually, out of a PR base of approximately 500,000.
Singapore Permanent Resident (Any Pathwa.pathToPR: N/A — you must already hold Singapore PR to follow this citizenship pathway.
Singapore Permanent Resident (Any Pathwa.pathToCitizenship: Hold Singapore PR → After 2+ years as PR, apply for citizenship by naturalization (ICA online portal) → ICA assessment (holistic, discretionary) → If approved: receive Citizenship Approval notification → Renounce all other nationalities → Take Oath of Renunciation, Allegiance and Loyalty → Receive Singapore identity card (NRIC) and passport → Singapore Citizen
Singapore Permanent Resident (Any Pathwa.timelineToCitizenship: 3–6 months processing after application (ICA processing time); 2+ years as PR required before applying
Singapore Permanent Resident (Any Pathwa.probabilityNote: Singapore does not publish citizenship approval rates. Based on ICA annual statistics (~18,000–23,000 new citizens per year from a PR base of ~500,000), approximately 3.5–4.5% of PRs naturalize annually. Among those who actually apply (not all PRs do), approval rates are estimated at 55–70% based on professional immigration advisors' accounts and community reports. The 62% reflects approved applications as a proportion of submitted applications. Key factors increasing approval: 10+ years Singapore residency, children who are Singapore citizens, CPF contributions, active community involvement, and strategic sector employment. The no-dual-citizenship rule significantly suppresses the number of eligible PRs who choose to apply. Source: ICA Singapore PR and Citizenship Annual Statistics.

Singapore Citizenship by Descent.description: A child born to at least one Singapore citizen parent acquires Singapore citizenship at birth, regardless of where the birth occurs. If born outside Singapore, the birth must be registered at a Singapore overseas mission (consulate/embassy) or at ICA within 12 months of birth to register the citizenship. IMPORTANT: Singapore does not recognize dual citizenship in adults. A child born abroad who acquires another citizenship by birth (e.g., born in the US and acquiring US citizenship by jus soli) holds dual citizenship until age 21, at which point they must formally choose Singapore citizenship and renounce the other — or lose their Singapore citizenship. Failure to make this choice is treated as renunciation of Singapore citizenship.
Singapore Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — Singapore citizenship by descent bypasses PR entirely.
Singapore Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm at least one parent was a Singapore citizen at time of birth → If born outside Singapore: register birth at Singapore overseas mission within 12 months → Receive Singapore birth certificate registration → Child is a Singapore citizen. → By age 21: formally renounce foreign nationality if holding dual citizenship (or lose Singapore citizenship by inaction)
Singapore Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth; overseas registration required within 12 months. Renunciation of other nationality required by age 21 for those born abroad.
Singapore Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: For those who genuinely qualify (born to a Singapore citizen parent), citizenship registration/confirmation has an approval rate of approximately 89–93%. The main reasons for non-confirmation: failure to register the birth overseas within the required 12-month window, the parent having renounced or lost Singapore citizenship before the child's birth, or questions about parentage. The 9% reduction also reflects children who fail to make the nationality choice by age 21 (effectively renouncing Singapore citizenship by inaction) or who voluntarily choose to retain their other nationality over Singapore citizenship. Source: ICA Singapore Nationality Act, consulate registration data.

permanentResidence.officialName: Niederlassungserlaubnis (Settlement Permit)
criteria: Have held a qualifying residence permit (work, Blue Card, family, etc.) for the required period (typically 5 years; 21 months for EU Blue Card holders with B1 German, 27 months without)
Have adequate knowledge of German (generally B1 CEFR level)
Have sufficient income to support yourself and dependants without relying on social benefits
Have made sufficient contributions to the German statutory pension scheme
Have accommodation adequate for yourself and any dependants
No serious criminal record or outstanding threats to public order
Pass the basic civic knowledge test (Leben in Deutschland) if required

routes.name: Student Visa → Opportunity Card / Job Seeker Route Pipeline
routes.description: International students graduate from a German university and use the 18-month job seeker visa to find qualifying work. Since 2024, Germany's Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte) allows skilled workers from non-EU countries to come to Germany to look for work without a prior job offer, based on a points system. After securing employment, the worker transitions to a Skilled Worker Visa or EU Blue Card, eventually reaching the Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubnis).
routes.estimatedDuration: ~5 years total: study years count toward the 5-year Settlement Permit threshold, so 3–4 years study + 1–2 years on a work permit or EU Blue Card (job seeker period ends the moment a job is found). EU Blue Card holders: Settlement Permit after 21 months on Blue Card (with B1 German) regardless of prior study time.

routes.name: EU Blue Card → Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubnis)
routes.description: The EU Blue Card is Germany's flagship route for highly qualified non-EU workers earning above the salary threshold (€45,300/year general; €35,100/year for shortage occupations as of 2024). Since the 2023 reform, Blue Card holders can apply for the Settlement Permit after just 27 months (or 21 months with B1 German). This is one of the fastest PR paths in Germany and grants rights to move to other EU member states.
routes.estimatedDuration: 21–27 months on EU Blue Card to Settlement Permit; add 6–12 months job search and application

routes.name: Skilled Worker Visa → Settlement Permit
routes.description: Germany's Fachkräfteeinwanderungsgesetz (Skilled Immigration Act), significantly expanded in 2023, allows non-EU workers with recognized qualifications (vocational or academic) to work in Germany without requiring a German employer to prove no EU worker is available. After 4 years on a work residence permit, workers can apply for the Settlement Permit, requiring sufficient German language and financial independence.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4–5 years on Skilled Worker Visa to Settlement Permit

routes.name: Family – Spouse / Family Reunification → Settlement Permit
routes.description: Spouses and minor children of German citizens or holders of the Settlement Permit can join them in Germany. Spouses of German citizens receive an initial 3-year residence permit and can apply for Settlement Permit after 3 years of living with their German-citizen spouse. Spouses of Settlement Permit holders follow the general 5-year route. Basic German language knowledge (A1) is required before entry for spouses joining German citizens.
routes.estimatedDuration: 3 years for spouses of German citizens; 5 years for family of permanent residents

routes.name: Freelance / Self-Employed Residence Permit → Settlement Permit
routes.description: Qualified freelancers (Freiberufler) in regulated professions — artists, journalists, scientists, doctors, lawyers, engineers — can receive a residence permit for self-employment. After 5 years of continuous self-employed residence with sufficient income and pension contributions, the Settlement Permit becomes available. Germany distinguishes between Freiberufler (professional freelancers) and Gewerbetreibende (business owners), with different requirements.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years of continuous qualifying self-employment + several months Settlement Permit processing

criteria: Have legally resided in Germany for at least 5 years (reduced to 3 years for special integration achievements under the 2024 reform)
Hold an unlimited or long-term residence permit (Settlement Permit or EU Long-Term Residence)
Be able to support yourself and dependants without social benefits (exceptions for reasons beyond your control)
Have adequate German language skills (B1 CEFR level minimum)
Pass the citizenship test (Einbürgerungstest) — 33 questions, 17 correct required
Have no serious criminal convictions
IMPORTANT: Germany now allows dual citizenship since the 2024 Nationality Act reform — you no longer need to renounce your existing nationality to become German

routes.name: Naturalization – General (5-Year Rule)
routes.description: After 5 years of legal residence in Germany (reduced from 8 under the 2024 reform), holders of a Settlement Permit or EU Long-Term Residence Permit can apply for citizenship. The 2024 Nationality Act also introduced a 3-year accelerated path for those with 'special integration achievements' (exceptional civic engagement, professional achievement, or voluntary work). Dual citizenship is now permitted since January 2024.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years legal residence (3 years with special achievements) + 12–18 months naturalization processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born to German Parent)
routes.description: A child born to at least one German citizen parent acquires German citizenship automatically at birth, regardless of where they are born (jus sanguinis). The child must be registered with a German consulate if born abroad. Since the 2024 reform, children born in Germany to foreign parents who have legally resided in Germany for 5+ years (previously 8) also acquire German citizenship at birth (jus soli).
routes.estimatedDuration: Automatic from birth; consular registration recommended within 1 year

German Student Visa.fullName: German Student Visa (Studienvisa) → Permanent Residence Pipeline
German Student Visa.description: The German Student Visa allows international students to study at recognized German universities and higher education institutions. Tuition fees at public universities are minimal (most charge only semester fees of €150–400). After graduation, students can stay 18 months on a job-seeker visa to find qualifying employment matching their degree. Germany actively encourages international graduates to remain, particularly in STEM and engineering fields. The pipeline leads from student visa → 18-month job seeker → Skilled Worker Visa or EU Blue Card → Settlement Permit.
German Student Visa.pathToPR: Student Visa → Study at German university (3–4 years — this time counts toward the 5-year qualifying residence total) → Graduate → Begin 18-month Job Seeker Visa → Find qualifying job offer → Immediately switch to Skilled Worker Visa or EU Blue Card (no need to wait out the full 18 months) → Complete remaining qualifying residence to reach 5 years total → Apply for Niederlassungserlaubnis (Settlement Permit). Example: 4 years study + 1 year on work permit = 5 years → Settlement Permit. Blue Card holders: 21 months on Blue Card (with B1 German) is sufficient, regardless of prior study residence.
German Student Visa.pathToCitizenship: Student Visa → Work on Blue Card or Skilled Worker Visa → After 5 years total lawful residence in Germany (study time counts) → Pass Einbürgerungstest (33 questions) + B1 German → Apply for Einbürgerung at local Ausländerbehörde → German Citizen. NOTE: The Settlement Permit is NOT required before citizenship — 5 years of any qualifying lawful residence (student + work) suffices. Alternatively: reach Settlement Permit threshold first (21–27 months Blue Card), then wait for 5 years total.
German Student Visa.timelineToPR: ~5 years total from starting studies: study time counts toward the 5-year threshold, so roughly 1–2 years of work/Blue Card time needed after a 3–4 year degree. Blue Card route: 21 months on Blue Card (B1 German) or 27 months without, from when Blue Card is issued.
German Student Visa.timelineToCitizenship: ~6–7 years from starting studies: 5 years total qualifying residence (study time counts) + 12–18 months naturalization processing. Settlement Permit is NOT a prerequisite — citizenship can be applied for directly at the 5-year mark.
German Student Visa.probabilityNote: Corrected timeline (5 years total, not 8–12): study time counts toward the 5-year Settlement Permit and citizenship threshold, materially shortening the pipeline and reducing attrition. The 35% PR probability reflects: ~55% of international graduates remain and seek employment; of those, ~70% secure qualifying work; of those, ~85% accumulate 5 qualifying years; ~90% PR approval rate. The 22% citizenship probability is higher than before the timeline correction because the 5-year total (not 13+) means fewer people leave before becoming eligible. Dual citizenship permitted since 2024 further raises naturalization take-up. Source: BAMF immigration statistics, 2024 Nationality Act data.

Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte).fullName: Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte) – Points-Based Job Seeker Visa
Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte).description: Introduced in June 2024 under Germany's revised Skilled Immigration Act, the Opportunity Card allows skilled workers from non-EU countries to come to Germany for up to 1 year to look for work — without a prior job offer. Eligibility is based on a points system: recognized qualification (professional or academic), German or English language skills, work experience, age, and prior Germany connection. Minimum 6 points required. Holders can work up to 20 hours/week in any job while searching. If they find qualifying work, they switch to a Skilled Worker Visa or EU Blue Card.
Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte).pathToPR: Qualify for Chancenkarte (6+ points) → Come to Germany (up to 1 year) → Find employer offering qualifying job → Switch to Skilled Worker Visa or EU Blue Card → After 4–5 years (or 21–27 months Blue Card) → Apply for Settlement Permit
Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte).pathToCitizenship: Chancenkarte → Work Visa → Settlement Permit → 5 years total legal residence → Naturalization → German Citizen
Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte).timelineToPR: 6–8 years from arriving on Chancenkarte: up to 1 year job search + 4–5 years on work permit (or 21–27 months Blue Card) to Settlement Permit
Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte).probabilityNote: Chancenkarte holders are pre-screened (6+ points, recognized qualification), increasing conversion to qualifying employment. The 42% PR probability reflects: ~65% find qualifying employment and switch to work permit; ~85% of those accumulate 5 qualifying years; ~90% approval rate. The 26% citizenship probability (raised from 22%) reflects the 2024 dual citizenship reform and the direct citizenship path at 5 years of total residence without requiring Settlement Permit first. Source: BAMF preliminary Chancenkarte statistics 2024.

EU Blue Card (Germany).description: Germany's most popular route for highly skilled non-EU workers. Requires a recognized academic degree (or 3+ years of equivalent professional experience accepted since 2023) and a job offer paying at least €45,300/year (general) or €35,100/year for shortage occupations (ICT, medicine, engineering, science — as of 2024 figures). The EU Blue Card is initially granted for 4 years (or job contract duration + 3 months). Key advantage: Settlement Permit eligibility after only 27 months (21 months with B1 German), the fastest PR path for non-EU workers in Germany.
EU Blue Card (Germany).pathToPR: Secure job offer meeting salary threshold → Apply for EU Blue Card → Work in Germany → After 21 months (B1 German) or 27 months → Apply for Niederlassungserlaubnis → Receive Settlement Permit
EU Blue Card (Germany).pathToCitizenship: EU Blue Card → After 5 years total lawful residence in Germany → Pass citizenship test + B1 German → Apply for Einbürgerung → German Citizen. The Settlement Permit is NOT required before citizenship — Blue Card holders with 5+ years of qualifying German residence (including any prior legal residence such as student permit time) can apply for citizenship directly. Alternatively: obtain Settlement Permit after 21–27 months on Blue Card, then apply for citizenship once 5 years total residence is reached.
EU Blue Card (Germany).timelineToPR: 21–27 months on EU Blue Card to Settlement Permit (fastest non-family PR in Germany)
EU Blue Card (Germany).timelineToCitizenship: ~5–7 years total lawful German residence (which can include prior student time) + 12–18 months naturalization processing. Blue Card holders do not need to obtain the Settlement Permit first — 5 years any qualifying residence → citizenship directly.
EU Blue Card (Germany).probabilityNote: EU Blue Card holders have a shorter, clearer path than previously described: Settlement Permit after 21–27 months, then citizenship from the 5-year total residence mark — with no requirement to hold the Settlement Permit before applying for citizenship. The 68% PR probability reflects strong employment stability among Blue Card holders; ~72% complete the 21–27-month Blue Card threshold; ~90% of qualifying applicants receive the Settlement Permit. The 50% citizenship probability (raised from 42%) reflects the more direct route and the 2024 dual citizenship reform eliminating the renunciation barrier. Source: BAMF EU Blue Card statistics.

Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).fullName: German Skilled Worker Visa (Fachkräfte-Einwanderungsgesetz) → Settlement Permit
Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).description: Under the reformed Skilled Immigration Act (Fachkräfteeinwanderungsgesetz) in force since November 2023, Germany significantly expanded pathways for non-EU skilled workers with recognized vocational or academic qualifications. No employer preference check (Vorrangprüfung) is required. Requirements: recognized qualification (professional or academic), job offer in the qualified field, salary appropriate for the occupation. The Skilled Worker Visa is the basis for the Settlement Permit after 4 years of qualifying work in Germany.
Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).pathToPR: Get qualification recognized by German authorities (e.g., ZAB for academic degrees, ANABIN database) → Secure job offer → Apply for Skilled Worker Visa at German embassy → Work in Germany → Renew visa → After 4 years qualifying work + B1 German + financial stability → Apply for Settlement Permit
Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).pathToCitizenship: Skilled Worker Visa → Settlement Permit → 5 years total legal residence → Citizenship test + B1 → Apply for naturalization → German Citizen
Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).timelineToPR: 4 years qualifying work residence + several months Settlement Permit processing
Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).timelineToCitizenship: 6–8 years from arriving in Germany to citizenship (5 years total legal residence required)
Skilled Worker Visa (Germany).probabilityNote: The 52% PR probability reflects: credential recognition success (~85%), visa approval (~90%), 4-year employment retention (~80%), Settlement Permit approval (~90%). The 33% citizenship probability (raised from 30%) reflects the 2024 dual citizenship reform and the fact that citizenship can be obtained at the 5-year total residence mark without requiring the Settlement Permit first. Source: BAMF Fachkräfteeinwanderung statistics 2023–2024.

Family Reunification Visa (Germany).fullName: German Family Reunification Visa → Settlement Permit
Family Reunification Visa (Germany).description: Family members of German citizens or holders of the Niederlassungserlaubnis can join them in Germany. Spouses joining German citizens need to demonstrate A1 German language ability before entry (an often-criticized requirement). The visa is initially a temporary residence permit; spouses of German citizens can apply for Settlement Permit after 3 years of living together in Germany (or immediately with the German-citizen spouse if the German citizen has been resident for 3+ years). Children under 16 do not need to show language ability.
Family Reunification Visa (Germany).pathToPR: German citizen/PR holder sponsors family member → Family member demonstrates A1 German (for spouses of German citizens) → Receive Family Reunification Visa → Reside in Germany with sponsor → Spouses of German citizens: apply for Settlement Permit after 3 years together in Germany. Spouses of PR holders: apply after 5 years total qualifying residence
Family Reunification Visa (Germany).pathToCitizenship: Family Reunification Visa → Settlement Permit → After 5 years total legal residence (or 3 with special achievements) → Citizenship test + B1 German → Apply for naturalization → German Citizen. Spouses of German citizens may have faster path depending on circumstances.
Family Reunification Visa (Germany).timelineToPR: 3 years for spouses of German citizens; 5 years for family of permanent residents + processing time
Family Reunification Visa (Germany).timelineToCitizenship: 5–7 years from arriving in Germany to citizenship eligibility
Family Reunification Visa (Germany).probabilityNote: Family reunification visa holders have a relatively high probability of reaching Settlement Permit status because their residence is tied to a stable family unit in Germany. The 72% reflects strong attrition mainly from relationship breakdown (divorce/separation leading to loss of entitlement) and some voluntary departures. Settlement Permit approval rate for qualifying applicants is approximately 90%. The 38% citizenship rate reflects that family members who arrive do eventually integrate and many naturalize, particularly since the 2024 dual citizenship reform. Source: BAMF family reunification statistics.

Freelance Visa (Germany).description: Germany's Freiberufler (freelancer) residence permit is available to professionals in regulated liberal arts and sciences: writers, artists, journalists, scientists, architects, doctors, lawyers, engineers, and teachers. Unlike other self-employed categories, Freiberufler are not registered with the trade office (Gewerbeamt). Requirements: demonstrated demand for services in Germany (client letters or contracts), ability to self-support financially, no public social benefit reliance. After 5 years of continuous freelance residence with sufficient income and pension contributions, the Settlement Permit is available.
Freelance Visa (Germany).pathToPR: Gather client letters/contracts → Apply for Freiberufler residence permit at German embassy → Arrive in Germany → Register with relevant professional body if required → File taxes annually, contribute to pension scheme → After 5 years + B1 German + financial stability → Apply for Settlement Permit
Freelance Visa (Germany).pathToCitizenship: Freelance Visa → Settlement Permit → 5 years total legal residence → Naturalization → German Citizen
Freelance Visa (Germany).timelineToPR: 5 years continuous qualifying freelance residence + several months Settlement Permit processing
Freelance Visa (Germany).probabilityNote: Freelancers face higher attrition than employed workers: income volatility, the challenge of maintaining self-sufficiency without social benefits over 5 continuous years, and the difficulty of demonstrating consistent demand for services. Approximately 40–50% of Freiberufler visa holders maintain qualifying status through to Settlement Permit eligibility. Of those who apply, approval is approximately 85%. Source: BAMF self-employment residence statistics.

Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubn.description: The Niederlassungserlaubnis (NE) is Germany's permanent residence status — an indefinite residence permit with no expiry, allowing holders to live and work freely in Germany. Holders can sponsor family members, access social benefits on the same terms as Germans, and travel freely within the Schengen Area. It is the direct stepping stone to citizenship. Since the 2024 Nationality Act reform, holders need only 5 years of total legal residence (3 with special achievements) before applying for naturalization, and may retain their original nationality.
Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubn.pathToPR: N/A — the Settlement Permit IS permanent residence in Germany.
Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubn.pathToCitizenship: NOTE: The Settlement Permit is NOT required before applying for German citizenship. Any holder of 5 years of qualifying lawful residence in Germany (regardless of visa type — student, Blue Card, Skilled Worker, etc.) can apply for citizenship directly. However, for those who do hold the Settlement Permit: Hold Settlement Permit → Ensure 5 years total legal residence in Germany → Pass Einbürgerungstest (33 questions) → Demonstrate B1 German → Apply for Einbürgerung at local Ausländerbehörde → Background check → Receive citizenship certificate → German Citizen. Accelerated path: 3 years residence for those with special integration achievements (2024 reform).
Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubn.timelineToCitizenship: 5 years total legal residence in Germany (3 years with special integration achievements) + 12–18 months naturalization processing
Settlement Permit (Niederlassungserlaubn.probabilityNote: Settlement Permit holders who apply for naturalization have an 85–90% approval rate; the 75% estimate accounts for eligible residents who do not apply (internationally mobile workers, those uncertain about staying). Since Germany now allows dual citizenship (2024 reform) and citizenship no longer requires holding the Settlement Permit first, naturalization rates are expected to rise significantly — Germany naturalized ~200,000 people in 2023, a record. Source: BAMF 2024 citizenship data.

German Citizenship by Descent.description: German citizenship is transmitted by descent (jus sanguinis) — a child born to at least one German citizen parent acquires German citizenship at birth, wherever the birth occurs. If born abroad, the birth should be registered at the nearest German consulate. Since the 2024 reform: (1) descendants of Germans who were forced to renounce their citizenship under the Nazi regime can now claim German citizenship; (2) children born in Germany to foreign parents who have legally resided in Germany for 5+ years now also acquire citizenship at birth (jus soli reduction from 8 to 5 years).
German Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — German citizenship by descent bypasses permanent residence entirely.
German Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm at least one parent was a German citizen at time of birth → If born abroad: register birth at German consulate → Child is a German citizen from birth. For Nazi-era descendants: gather documentation proving persecution-based renunciation → Apply under § 5 StAG or via the new §§ 14a–15 StAG (2024 reform) → Receive citizenship
German Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth. Consular birth registration recommended within 1 year. Nazi-era descendant claims: 6–24 months processing depending on documentation.
German Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: For those born to a German citizen parent, citizenship acquisition is automatic — the 92% (rather than 100%) accounts for edge cases: births abroad not registered within required timeframes, rare disputes over parentage, and the small fraction who renounce German citizenship voluntarily later in life. For Nazi-era descendant claims, approval rates are high (~85–90%) for well-documented cases. Source: German Federal Foreign Office citizenship statistics.

permanentResidence.officialName: Carte de Résident (10-Year Residence Card)
criteria: Have lived continuously and legally in France for at least 5 years on a valid residence permit
Demonstrate integration into French society (knowledge of French language at B1 level minimum, adherence to republican values)
Have sufficient stable resources to support yourself (typically at least the French minimum wage, SMIC)
Not have been subject to any deportation order or serious criminal conviction
Pass an integration interview (entretien d'intégration civique) if requested by the prefecture

routes.name: Student Visa → Talent Passport Pipeline
routes.description: International students who graduate from a French institution (master's or PhD) can apply for the Autorisation Provisoire de Séjour (APS) — a 1-year permit to seek work or start a business. After finding qualifying employment, they transition to the Talent Passport (Passeport Talent), a 4-year multi-entry residence permit covering highly skilled workers, researchers, and startup founders. After 5 cumulative years on qualifying permits, the Carte de Résident is available.
routes.estimatedDuration: ~5 years total: study time counts toward the 5-year Carte de Résident residence threshold. Example: 3 years study + 2 years qualifying work (APS job search ends the moment a job is found) = 5 years → Carte de Résident. Graduates of French grandes écoles can naturalize after just 2 more years of qualifying residence (5 total).

routes.name: Talent Passport (Passeport Talent) → Carte de Résident
routes.description: The Talent Passport (Passeport Talent) is a 4-year residence permit for highly skilled workers, researchers, artists, company executives, startup founders, and investors. It requires either a job offer with a salary of at least 1.5× the average annual salary (~€53,000+) or qualification-based eligibility. After 5 cumulative years of legal residence in France on qualifying permits, the holder can apply for the Carte de Résident.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years of cumulative legal residence in France to Carte de Résident

routes.name: Work Visa (Salarié) → Carte de Résident
routes.description: Standard long-stay work visa for non-EU/EEA nationals with a French employer sponsoring them. Requires a work contract, employer sponsorship, and OFII validation. The work visa is renewed annually; after 5 continuous years of legal residence, the holder can apply for the Carte de Résident, which is valid for 10 years and renewable.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years continuous legal residence on work visas + several months Carte de Résident processing

routes.name: Family – Regroupement Familial → Carte de Résident
routes.description: Spouses and minor children of French citizens or long-term residents can join them through the regroupement familial procedure. A spouse of a French citizen receives a 'vie privée et familiale' card (1-year initially, then 3-year) and can apply for the Carte de Résident after 5 years, or can naturalize after 4 years of marriage. Spouses of French citizens have an accelerated path compared to other categories.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4 years of marriage to a French citizen for naturalization eligibility; 5 years for general Carte de Résident

routes.name: Investor – Exceptional Economic Contribution → Carte de Résident
routes.description: France's investor pathway falls under the Talent Passport category for investors making a 'significant economic contribution' to France: investing at least €300,000 in productive assets in France and creating or preserving at least 10 jobs. The visa is granted for 4 years; after 5 years of qualifying residence, the investor can apply for the Carte de Résident.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4-year Talent Passport Investor; 5 years total for Carte de Résident eligibility

criteria: Have lived continuously in France for at least 5 years (2 years if graduated from a French grandes écoles or similar institution; 2 years for exceptional service to France)
Be at least 18 years old
Demonstrate assimilation into French culture: language level B1 minimum (tested), knowledge of French rights and duties, and attachment to French values
Have stable and sufficient income
Not have been convicted of a crime or offence incompatible with French nationality
Not have been subject to a deportation or exclusion order in the 10 years preceding the application
France generally permits dual citizenship — you do not need to renounce your existing nationality

routes.name: Naturalization – General (5-Year Rule)
routes.description: After 5 years of continuous and regular residence in France, any legally resident foreigner can apply for French citizenship by naturalization (décret de naturalisation). The prefecture conducts an assimilation interview to assess French language ability, knowledge of French institutions and values, and integration into French society. Processing takes 12–18 months. France allows dual citizenship.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years residence in France + 12–18 months naturalization processing

routes.name: Naturalization via Marriage to French Citizen (4-Year Rule)
routes.description: Spouses of French citizens can apply for naturalization after 4 years of marriage and continuous residence in France (5 years if not meeting language requirements). The period is reduced to 3 years if they have a child with French citizenship or lived abroad representing France. Community of life and assimilation are assessed.
routes.estimatedDuration: 4 years of marriage + qualifying residence + 12–18 months processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born to French Parent)
routes.description: A child born to at least one French citizen parent is automatically a French citizen at birth, regardless of birthplace (jus sanguinis). France also has jus soli rules for children born in France to foreign parents who have lived in France for 5 years. Children born in France who have not acquired another citizenship can claim French citizenship at age 18. France permits dual citizenship.
routes.estimatedDuration: Automatic from birth; passport application 2–6 months

Student Visa (France).fullName: French Long-Stay Student Visa (VLS-TS Étudiant) → Carte de Résident Pipeline
Student Visa (France).description: France's long-stay student visa (visa long séjour valant titre de séjour — VLS-TS étudiant) allows international students to study at accredited French institutions without obtaining a separate residence permit in the first year. France has strong universities including the grandes écoles (HEC, Polytechnique, Sciences Po) and is a target for international students — Campus France coordinates admissions. After graduating with a master's or PhD, students can apply for the APS (Autorisation Provisoire de Séjour) for 1 year to find work. The pipeline leads to Talent Passport → Carte de Résident → citizenship.
Student Visa (France).pathToPR: Student Visa → Study at French institution (2–3 years — this time counts toward the 5-year Carte de Résident threshold) → Graduate → Apply for APS (1-year job-seeker permit, ends the moment a qualifying job is found) → Immediately switch to Talent Passport or Work Visa → Accumulate remaining qualifying residence to reach 5 years total → Apply for Carte de Résident. Example: 3 years study + APS (job found quickly) + 1–2 years Talent Passport = 5 years.
Student Visa (France).pathToCitizenship: Student Visa → APS → Talent Passport or Work Visa → Carte de Résident → After 5 years total qualifying residence (or 2 years if French grandes écoles graduate) → Naturalization → French Citizen
Student Visa (France).timelineToPR: ~5 years total from starting studies: study time counts toward the 5-year Carte de Résident threshold, so only 1–2 years of post-graduation qualifying work are needed after a 3-year degree. APS job-search permit ends immediately when a job is found.
Student Visa (France).timelineToCitizenship: ~6–8 years: 5 years total qualifying residence (PR eligibility) + 12–18 months naturalization processing. Accelerated: graduates of French grandes écoles or equivalent can naturalize after 2 years of qualifying residence — so ~5 years from starting studies + 12–18 months processing.
Student Visa (France).probabilityNote: Corrected timeline (~5 years, not 7–10): study time counts toward the 5-year Carte de Résident threshold. With the shorter pipeline, attrition is significantly lower. The 40% PR probability reflects: ~50% of graduates remain and seek work; ~70% find qualifying employment; ~85% accumulate 5 qualifying years; ~90% Carte de Résident approval. The 25% citizenship probability reflects France's accessible naturalization (dual citizenship permitted, 5-year residence threshold aligns with PR), partially offset by the assimilation interview requirement. Source: Ministère de l'Intérieur immigration statistics, OFII data.

Talent Passport (France).fullName: French Talent Passport (Passeport Talent) → Carte de Résident
Talent Passport (France).description: The Talent Passport is France's flagship permit for attracting skilled foreign talent — researchers, artists, company executives, innovative startup founders, and high-earning employees. It covers 10 sub-categories. Key ones: (1) Employees earning ≥1.5× average salary (~€53,000+) with a higher-education qualification; (2) Researchers with hosting agreement from French research institution; (3) Innovative company founders (young innovative company label); (4) Investors (€300,000+ + 10 jobs created/maintained); (5) Artists and cultural figures. Valid for 4 years (renewable), with open work rights for the spouse.
Talent Passport (France).pathToPR: Qualify for one Talent Passport sub-category → Apply at French consulate → Receive 4-year Talent Passport → Work/reside in France → Renew if needed → After 5 cumulative years qualifying residence → Apply for Carte de Résident at prefecture
Talent Passport (France).pathToCitizenship: Talent Passport → 5 years residence → Carte de Résident → Naturalization application → Assimilation interview → French Citizen (dual nationality permitted)
Talent Passport (France).timelineToPR: 5 years total qualifying residence in France (the Talent Passport covers 4 years of that)
Talent Passport (France).timelineToCitizenship: 6–8 years from first arriving on Talent Passport (5 years residence + 12–18 months naturalization processing)
Talent Passport (France).probabilityNote: Talent Passport holders are pre-screened for economic contribution; most who remain 5 years qualify for and receive the Carte de Résident. The 62% PR probability reflects strong but not universal retention (globally mobile professionals may leave). The 40% citizenship probability reflects solid naturalization rates — France naturalizes ~110,000 people annually and dual citizenship is permitted. Source: Ministère de l'Intérieur DGEF statistics.

Work Visa (France).fullName: French Long-Stay Work Visa (Visa Salarié) → Carte de Résident
Work Visa (France).description: The standard work visa for non-EU/EEA nationals employed by a French company. Requires an employment contract, employer sponsorship, and validation by OFII (Office Français de l'Immigration et de l'Intégration). The employer must typically demonstrate that no EU/EEA candidate is available (opposabilité de la situation de l'emploi) — though exceptions exist for shortage occupations. Annual renewal required. After 5 continuous years of legal qualifying residence, the Carte de Résident (10-year renewable) becomes available.
Work Visa (France).pathToPR: Secure French employer → Employer applies for work authorization → Apply for long-stay work visa → Arrive in France → Complete OFII integration procedure → Renew annually → After 5 years continuous qualifying residence → Apply for Carte de Résident
Work Visa (France).pathToCitizenship: Work Visa → 5 years → Carte de Résident → Naturalization (5 years total, B1 French, assimilation interview) → French Citizen
Work Visa (France).timelineToPR: 5 years continuous legal residence in France + several months Carte de Résident processing
Work Visa (France).probabilityNote: The corrected 5-year timeline (study time counts) reduces attrition for students who transition to work visas. For direct work visa arrivals (no prior student time), the 5-year qualifying period applies from arrival. The 55% PR probability reflects: ~65% maintain qualifying employment for 5 years; ~88% Carte de Résident approval. The 32% citizenship probability reflects France's accessible naturalization process. Source: DGEF annual immigration report.

Family Reunification Visa (France).fullName: French Family Reunification Visa (Regroupement Familial) → Carte de Résident
Family Reunification Visa (France).description: Spouses and dependent children of French citizens or legally resident foreigners can join them through regroupement familial. Spouses of French citizens receive a 'vie privée et familiale' residence card and have an accelerated path to both Carte de Résident and naturalization. Spouses of resident foreigners must go through the prefecture-managed regroupement familial procedure (minimum income and housing requirements apply). Family reunion with French citizens is generally more streamlined than with foreign residents.
Family Reunification Visa (France).pathToPR: French citizen/resident sponsors family member → Prefectural or consular application → Receive family visa → Complete OFII integration procedure → Receive initial residence card → Renew → After 5 years qualifying residence (spouses of French citizens may apply for Carte de Résident earlier based on circumstances) → Carte de Résident
Family Reunification Visa (France).pathToCitizenship: Family Visa → Carte de Résident → Naturalization. Spouses of French citizens: after 4 years of marriage and qualifying residence → naturalization. Others: 5 years residence → naturalization.
Family Reunification Visa (France).timelineToPR: 5 years qualifying residence; earlier in some cases for spouses of French citizens
Family Reunification Visa (France).timelineToCitizenship: 4–6 years for spouses of French citizens (4-year marriage route); 6–8 years for others
Family Reunification Visa (France).probabilityNote: Family reunification visa holders have the highest probability of long-term settlement in France due to the anchor of family ties. Approximately 75–80% accumulate the required residence; prefecture approval rates for Carte de Résident are approximately 90%. The 42% citizenship rate reflects solid naturalization rates among settled family members. Source: Ministère de l'Intérieur immigration and naturalization statistics.

Investor Visa (France).fullName: French Talent Passport – Investor Category → Carte de Résident
Investor Visa (France).description: France's investor immigration path falls within the Talent Passport framework. To qualify as an investor: commit at least €300,000 in real and productive assets in a French company (or purchase shares in an existing French company) AND create or preserve at least 10 jobs in France within 4 years. French nationality is available after 5 years of qualifying residence. Unlike pure golden visa programs, France requires active job creation, not just passive investment.
Investor Visa (France).pathToPR: Commit €300,000 investment in French productive assets + commit to 10 jobs created/maintained → Apply for Talent Passport (Investor) → 4-year permit → Document investment and job creation → After 5 years qualifying residence → Carte de Résident
Investor Visa (France).pathToCitizenship: Talent Passport (Investor) → 5 years residence → Carte de Résident → Naturalization → French Citizen
Investor Visa (France).timelineToCitizenship: 6–8 years from initial investment to citizenship
Investor Visa (France).probabilityNote: Investor category Talent Passport holders who maintain their investment and job creation commitments have a relatively high probability of reaching Carte de Résident. The 38% citizenship probability reflects that many investor-class residents are globally mobile and may not prioritize naturalization. Source: DGEF investor visa statistics.

Carte de Résident (France).fullName: French Carte de Résident (10-Year Residence Card) → Citizenship
Carte de Résident (France).description: The Carte de Résident is France's permanent residence equivalent — a 10-year renewable residence card that grants free access to the French labor market, social benefits, and the right to sponsor family members. Holders may naturalize after 5 years of total legal residence in France (which typically means they already hold the Carte de Résident). France generally permits dual citizenship — there is no requirement to renounce your original nationality upon naturalization.
Carte de Résident (France).pathToPR: N/A — the Carte de Résident IS permanent residence in France.
Carte de Résident (France).pathToCitizenship: Hold Carte de Résident → Ensure 5 years total legal residence in France → Prepare naturalization dossier (proof of income, housing, language B1, integration) → Submit to prefecture → Assimilation interview → Ministerial decree (décret de naturalisation) → French Citizen. Ceremony optional.
Carte de Résident (France).timelineToCitizenship: 5 years total qualifying residence in France + 12–18 months naturalization processing
Carte de Résident (France).probabilityNote: Among Carte de Résident holders who decide to apply for naturalization, France's approval rate is approximately 70–75% at first application (some are deferred due to language or integration concerns and reapply). The 68% accounts for both those who apply and the subset who remain long-term residents without naturalizing. France naturalized approximately 110,000–120,000 people annually in recent years. Source: Ministère de l'Intérieur naturalization statistics.

Spouse of French Citizen Visa.fullName: Spouse of French Citizen → Naturalization (4-Year Marriage Route)
Spouse of French Citizen Visa.description: Spouses of French citizens benefit from an accelerated naturalization path: after 4 years of marriage and continuous qualifying residence in France, they can apply for naturalization (the standard 5-year residence requirement is reduced). The period is reduced to 3 years if they have a child with French citizenship or if they lived 3 years abroad in a French overseas territory or worked for a French government mission. Community of life (living together) with the French spouse is required; the period is suspended if the couple separates.
Spouse of French Citizen Visa.pathToPR: N/A — the primary benefit here is accelerated citizenship, not a separate PR path. Spouses of French citizens receive a 'vie privée et familiale' card, not a direct PR equivalent.
Spouse of French Citizen Visa.pathToCitizenship: Marry French citizen → Obtain 'vie privée et familiale' residence card → Reside in France for 4 years of marriage (continuous community of life) → Prepare naturalization dossier → Prefecture interview assessing language (B1) and integration → Ministerial decree → French Citizen
Spouse of French Citizen Visa.timelineToCitizenship: 4 years of marriage and qualifying residence + 12–18 months processing (minimum 5–6 years from marriage date)
Spouse of French Citizen Visa.probabilityNote: Spouses of French citizens applying through the 4-year marriage route have a higher approval rate than general naturalization applicants because the France ties are more established (living with a French citizen). Approval rates for this category are approximately 75–80%, with deferrals mainly for insufficient French language or demonstrated integration. Source: Ministère de l'Intérieur naturalization by category statistics.

French Citizenship by Descent.description: A child born to at least one French citizen parent is automatically French at birth by filiation, regardless of birthplace. France also has jus soli provisions: children born in France to foreign parents who have resided in France for at least 5 years can claim French citizenship at age 18 (or earlier under certain conditions), and children born in France who have not acquired another citizenship are French. France permits dual citizenship — French citizens by descent are not required to renounce other nationalities.
French Citizenship by Descent.pathToPR: N/A — French citizenship by descent bypasses permanent residence entirely.
French Citizenship by Descent.pathToCitizenship: Confirm at least one parent was a French citizen at time of birth → Child is French at birth → Register with French consulate if born abroad → Apply for French birth certificate and passport. For jus soli: be born in France + at least one parent resided in France 5+ years → Claim citizenship at 18 (or earlier) at the prefecture.
French Citizenship by Descent.timelineToCitizenship: Citizenship exists from birth for children of French citizens. Consular birth registration: 2–12 months for passport.
French Citizenship by Descent.probabilityNote: For children genuinely born to a French citizen parent, citizenship acquisition is essentially automatic — the 94% accounts for rare procedural failures (birth not registered within required timeframes abroad, disputed parentage) and the small fraction who voluntarily renounce French citizenship. Source: Ministère des Affaires Étrangères consular statistics.

permanentResidence.officialName: EU Long-Term Residence Permit (Verblijfsvergunning voor onbepaalde tijd EU)
criteria: Have legally and continuously resided in the Netherlands for at least 5 years
Have a sufficient, independent, and durable income (at least 100% of the applicable social assistance norm)
Pass the civic integration exam (inburgeringsexamen) at B1 level in Dutch language and knowledge of Dutch society — or hold an exemption
No threats to public order or national security
Have had valid health insurance throughout the qualifying period
IMPORTANT: The Netherlands generally does NOT permit dual nationality — acquiring Dutch citizenship typically requires renouncing other nationalities (with limited exceptions)

routes.name: Student Permit → Orientation Year → Highly Skilled Migrant Pipeline
routes.description: International students who complete a bachelor's, master's, or PhD at a Dutch university can apply for the Orientation Year (Zoekjaar Hoogopgeleide) — a 1-year permit to find work matching their degree. If they find employment paying above the Highly Skilled Migrant (HSM) salary threshold (€4,840/month in 2024 for those 30+; €3,549/month under 30), they transition to the HSM permit. After 5 years of qualifying residence including student and HSM time, the EU Long-Term Residence Permit is available.
routes.estimatedDuration: ~5 years total: student permit time counts toward the 5-year EU Long-Term Residence threshold. Example: 3–4 years study + Orientation Year (ends when HSM-qualifying job is found) + remaining time to reach 5 years total. Finding a job quickly during the Orientation Year shortens the overall timeline significantly.

routes.name: Highly Skilled Migrant Permit → EU Long-Term Residence
routes.description: The Highly Skilled Migrant (HSM / Kennismigrant) permit is the Netherlands' flagship route for skilled non-EU workers, requiring a salary above the IND threshold (€4,840/month for those 30+; €3,549/month for those under 30; €2,801/month for recent Dutch university graduates in 2024). No labour market test required. The HSM permit is employer-tied but can be changed between recognised sponsors. After 5 years of continuous legal residence in the Netherlands, the EU Long-Term Residence Permit is available.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years of qualifying residence (HSM permit time counts) + several months processing

routes.name: EU Blue Card → EU Long-Term Residence
routes.description: The EU Blue Card in the Netherlands is available to non-EU nationals with a higher education degree and a salary of at least €5,670/month (2024). It is similar to the HSM permit but carries EU-wide mobility rights. After 5 years of legal residence in the Netherlands (EU Blue Card time counts toward this), the EU Long-Term Residence Permit is available.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years of qualifying residence + processing

routes.name: Family – Spouse/Partner Reunification → EU Long-Term Residence
routes.description: Spouses and registered partners of Dutch citizens or holders of a valid Dutch residence permit can join them in the Netherlands. An income requirement applies (sponsor must earn at least 100% of the applicable minimum wage). The family member receives an initial temporary permit; after 5 years of continuous lawful residence, the EU Long-Term Residence Permit becomes available.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years continuous qualifying residence + processing

routes.name: Self-Employed Permit → EU Long-Term Residence
routes.description: Non-EU entrepreneurs can obtain a self-employed residence permit if their business plan meets the IND's points-based assessment (based on personal experience, business plan viability, and added value for the Netherlands). After 5 years of continuous qualifying residence as a self-employed person with sufficient income, the EU Long-Term Residence Permit becomes available.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years qualifying self-employment residence + processing

criteria: Have held a permanent or long-term residence permit and have legally and continuously resided in the Netherlands for at least 5 years
Have successfully passed the civic integration exam (inburgeringsexamen) at B1 level — or hold an exemption (e.g., Dutch diploma, refugee status)
Be of good conduct (no serious criminal convictions in the past 4 years)

IMPORTANT: The Netherlands generally requires renunciation of other nationalities upon naturalization. Exceptions include: people from countries that do not allow renunciation, stateless persons, spouses/registered partners of Dutch citizens (if renunciation would cause serious problems), and those who lost Dutch citizenship through marriage
Be at least 16 years old

routes.name: Naturalization – General (5-Year Rule)
routes.description: After 5 years of continuous and lawful residence in the Netherlands, holders of a long-term or permanent residence permit can apply for Dutch citizenship by naturalization (naturalisatie). The civic integration requirement (B1 Dutch + knowledge of Dutch society) must be fulfilled. The Netherlands generally requires renunciation of other nationalities; applicants from countries that prohibit renunciation are exempt.
routes.estimatedDuration: 5 years qualifying residence + 12–24 months naturalization processing

routes.name: Citizenship by Descent (Born to Dutch Parent)
routes.description: A child born to at least one Dutch parent acquires Dutch citizenship at birth (jus sanguinis), regardless of where the birth occurs. Children born in the Netherlands to foreign parents who have resided legally in the Netherlands for 5 years may also acquire Dutch citizenship. Dutch citizenship acquired by descent can be passed to the next generation only if the child is born in the Netherlands or is registered as Dutch before age 7.
routes.estimatedDuration: Automatic from birth; passport application 2–6 months

Student Permit (Netherlands).fullName: Dutch Student Permit (Verblijfsvergunning Studie) → EU Long-Term Residence Pipeline
Student Permit (Netherlands).description: The Netherlands does not issue a separate student visa for non-EU nationals studying at Dutch universities — instead, the university acts as the IND-recognised sponsor and requests the student's residence permit (verblijfsvergunning). The Netherlands has strong research universities (TU Delft, Wageningen, Leiden, Maastricht) with many English-language programs. After graduation, students can apply for the Orientation Year (Zoekjaar Hoogopgeleide) to find qualifying employment. The pipeline: Student Permit → Orientation Year → HSM Permit → EU Long-Term Residence.
Student Permit (Netherlands).pathToPR: Student Permit → Study at Dutch university (3–4 years — this time counts toward the 5-year EU Long-Term Residence threshold) → Graduate → Apply for 1-year Orientation Year Permit → Find HSM-qualifying job (permit ends immediately when job is found — no need to wait out the full year) → Switch to HSM Permit → Accumulate remaining qualifying residence to reach 5 years total → Apply for EU Long-Term Residence Permit. Example: 4 years study + Orientation Year (job found at 3 months) = 4.25 years, then ~1 more year on HSM = 5 years → PR.
Student Permit (Netherlands).pathToCitizenship: Student Permit → HSM Permit → EU Long-Term Residence → Pass civic integration exam (B1 Dutch) → Apply for naturalization → Renounce other nationalities (generally required) → Dutch Citizen
Student Permit (Netherlands).timelineToPR: ~5 years total from starting studies: student permit time counts toward the 5-year threshold. After a 3–4 year degree, only 1–2 years of HSM permit time are needed to reach 5 years. The Orientation Year ends the moment qualifying employment is found.
Student Permit (Netherlands).timelineToCitizenship: ~6–8 years: 5 years qualifying residence (same years count for both EU Long-Term Residence and citizenship) + 12–24 months naturalization processing. Renunciation of other nationality generally required, which significantly affects decision to apply.
Student Permit (Netherlands).probabilityNote: Corrected timeline (~5 years, not 8–11): student permit time counts toward the 5-year EU Long-Term Residence threshold. With the shorter pipeline, fewer people leave before reaching eligibility. The 42% PR probability reflects: ~55% of graduates apply for and receive the Orientation Year; ~65% find HSM-qualifying employment; ~85% accumulate 5 qualifying years; ~90% EU Long-Term Residence approval. The 16% citizenship probability remains low — the Netherlands' renunciation requirement (generally must give up other nationality) is a major barrier that the shorter timeline does not remove. Source: IND annual statistics.

Orientation Year Permit (Netherlands).fullName: Dutch Orientation Year Permit (Zoekjaar Hoogopgeleide) → HSM Permit
Orientation Year Permit (Netherlands).description: The Orientation Year (Zoekjaar Hoogopgeleide) is a 1-year residence permit allowing recent graduates of Dutch or top-100 international universities to seek employment in the Netherlands. Eligibility: must have obtained a bachelor's, master's, or PhD within the past 3 years. Holders may work in any job during the year.
